article_metadata.title: Development of Bilayer Tablets with Modified Release of Selected Incompatible Drugs
article_metadata.objective: Develop bilayer tablets with conventional release TMS and slow-release SVT to co-formulate incompatible drugs safely in one tablet.

key_outcomes.summary: TMS layer achieved ≥85% release by 45 min; SVT layer achieved ~80% by 8 h with no SVT release in first hour at pH 1.2.
key_outcomes.best_formulation_note: 001_F4 gave the highest TMS 1 h release and acceptable SVT control; maps to 001_T4/001_TMS4/001_SVT4 in this schema.

APIs.telmisartan: Gift sample, Medley Pharmaceuticals Ltd., Daman, India
APIs.simvastatin: Gift sample, Lincoln Pharmaceuticals Ltd., Ahmedabad, India

excipients.croscarmellose_sodium: SD Fine Chem. Ltd., Mumbai, India
excipients.HPMC_K15M: SD Fine Chem. Ltd., Mumbai, India
excipients.PVP_K30: Central Drug House (P) Ltd., Mumbai, India
excipients.aerosil: Listed in TMS layer; commercial source not specified

experimental_design_for_optimization.design_type: Eight trial formulations per layer (001_F1–F8) varying superdisintegrant in TMS layer; binder type/level, HPMC/Carbopol, croscarmellose/DCP in SVT layer; combined to bilayer tablets 001_F1–F8.
response_variables: Granule flow indices (bulk/tapped density, Hausner ratio, Carr’s index, angle of repose)
Tablet hardness, friability, thickness, weight variation, layer separation, disintegration
Dissolution profiles in 0.1M HCl (1 h) then pH 6.8 buffer (7 h)
Release kinetics fits (zero, first order, Higuchi, Peppas) and ANOVA

analysis_methods.statistics: Two-way ANOVA with Bonferroni post-tests; p < 0.0001 threshold

FTIR.compatibility_findings: Layers individually show preserved API characteristic peaks; physical mixture of layers shows reduced intensity/shifted/disappeared peaks, evidencing incompatibility when blended.

equations.tapped_density: \(Tapped density = \frac{Weight of granules}{Final volume after tapping}\)

granulation.sieving: #80 pre-sift; wet mass through #16; dried mass through #22
granulation.binders: SVT layer: starch paste (2–5% w/v) or starch powder

compression.press: 10-station rotary press (001_M26 001_A12, Karnavati Engineering Ltd., Ahmedabad)

testing_equipment.dissolution: USP II (DS 8000), 900 mL, 100 rpm, 37 ± 0.5°C; 0.1M HCl 1 h then pH 6.8

method_overview.sequence: 0.1M HCl (pH 1.2) for 1 h then phosphate buffer (pH 6.8) for 7 h at 100 rpm, 37 ± 0.5°C
method_overview.findings_summary: No SVT release in first hour; TMS shows rapid release; SVT shows controlled release to ~80% by 8 h depending on batch.

TMS_release_highlights.note: TMS release increased with higher croscarmellose; 001_F4 (001_TMS4) achieved 93.84 ± 0.99% at 1 h.
TMS_release_highlights.criterion: Conventional release target >80% in 45 min satisfied.

release_mechanism_interpretation.model_fit: SVT release best fits first-order and Higuchi models across batches.
release_mechanism_interpretation.mechanism: Diffusion-controlled release with swelling; Peppas n between 0.5 and 1 indicates Fickian diffusion with swelling.

two_way_anova.significance: Significant differences among SVT dissolution profiles 001_F1–F8; polymer composition significantly affects release.

disintegration.TMS_layer: 4–15 min depending on batch and croscarmellose level
disintegration.SVT_layer: Not fully disintegrated within 2 h for all batches

section_locator.abstract: Study aim, design summary, FTIR compatibility statement, and headline dissolution/outcomes.
section_locator.introduction: Rationale for bilayer approach with TMS/SVT, incompatibility context, therapeutic logic.
section_locator.materials: API and excipient sources; instruments listed.
section_locator.methods: Granulation, drying, compression, instruments, test conditions, equations; Tables 1–2 compositions.
section_locator.results_discussion: Preformulation (melting point, UV λmax), FTIR assignments (Table 3), micromeritic data (Tables 4–5), tablet tests (Tables 6–8), dissolution and kinetics (Figures 4–5, Table 9), ANOVA (Table 10).
section_locator.conclusion: Best batch summary and mechanism statement; compliance benefits.

Materials_origin.acarbose: Ranbaxy Research Laboratories Ltd., Gurgaon, India [file:1]
Materials_origin.fenugreek_gum: Emerald Seed Products Ltd., Saskatchewan, Canada [file:1]
Materials_origin.talc: S.D. Fine Chemicals Ltd., Mumbai, India [file:1]
Materials_origin.magnesium_stearate: S.D. Fine Chemicals Ltd., Mumbai, India [file:1]

Experimental_design_for_optimization.approach: Matrix tablets with different ratios of gums; direct compression for tablet formation; factorial design for evaluating release control [file:1]
Experimental_design_for_optimization.animal_model: Male Wistar rats, high-fructose diet induced Type II diabetes; 5 groups: control, diabetic, IR treated, CR treated, placebo [file:1]

ingredients_identification_characterizat.preparation: Direct compression of physical mixtures, geometric addition, blending, and compression into tablets using single-punch tableting machine [file:1]
ingredients_identification_characterizat.results: Tablets meet IP requirements. Drug content: 99.01-100.70%. Validation RP-HPLC (λ=210 nm); FTIR: no interaction between drug and polymers (bands: 3371, 1652, 1418, 1152 cm⁻¹) [file:1]
ingredients_identification_characterizat.absorbance: UV spectrophotometer used at λmax 210 nm for drug determination [file:1]

Fourier_transform_infrared_spectral_meas.findings: No significant interaction between acarbose and gums; major peaks for OH, CC, CO and water stretching confirmed [file:1]

Tablet_fabrications_and_evaluation.equipment: Single-station tablet machine (Modern Engineering, New Delhi), biconcave die punch 9.7 mm diameter [file:1]

process_parameters.pressure: Not specified, but using biconcave punch set [file:1]

Tablet_fabrications_and_evaluation.batch_evaluations: Physical parameters (weight variation, thickness, hardness, friability, drug content) as per pharmacopoeial standards [file:1]

stability.002_AF40: No significant change after 6 months; f2=83 (>50); shelf life 1.47 years [file:1]

swelling_index.002_AF40–002_AF60: Swelling proportional to fenugreek gum concentration, max at 12h, with gel formation; ALBG/AB: initial swelling, then erosion or fragmentation [file:1]

in_vitro.release: See release profiles; kinetic models applied (first order, Higuchi, Korsmeyer-Peppas, Hixson Crowell)
in_vitro.swelling_water_uptake: 002_AF40–002_AF60: lag ~30min, max swelling at 12h; others erode quicker [file:1]

in_vivo.animal_model: Wistar rats; 002_AF40 selected; significant decrease in postprandial blood glucose and triglycerides; placebo (fenugreek gum) effective alone [file:1]
in_vivo.body_weight: Reduction in weight gain in sustained release group; 002_AF40 body weight decreased from 84.33±3.35 to 66.78±5.78 g in 5 days [file:1]
in_vivo.side_effects: Occurrence of diarrhea lowered with sustained release formulation; food intake increased (002_AF40 group ate 80–95g in 72h) [file:1]

Experimental_data.statistics: Significance stated as p<0.001 for glucose, triglycerides, body weight [file:1]

Polyethylene_oxide.PEO_750: Polyox WSR 750, MW = 3x10^5, manufactured by Dow Chemical (Philadelphia, USA), distributed by Colorcon (Kent, UK)
Polyethylene_oxide.PEO_303: Polyox WSR 303, MW = 7x10^6, manufactured by Dow Chemical (Philadelphia, USA), distributed by Colorcon (Kent, UK)

Materials_origin.Lactose: α-lactose monohydrate, donated by DMV International, Netherlands

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Materials were individually weighed and mixed for 10 minutes (except magnesium stearate) using a TURBULA blender at 100 rpm. Magnesium stearate was then mixed with the final mixture for an additional 2 minutes.
ingredients_identification.results: The study identified that smaller PEO particle sizes and higher molecular weight (PEO 303) resulted in harder tablets and slower drug release. Larger particle sizes and lower molecular weight (PEO 750) led to faster release and higher porosity.
ingredients_identification.absorbance: UV spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) at 271 nm for theophylline.

003_An-Investigation-on-the-Effect-of-Po.Fourier_Transform_Infrared_Spectral_Meas: Not available in the provided text.

Tablet_Fabrications.Process: A specific amount (350 mg) of the blended powder containing 1% magnesium stearate was compressed using an ENERPA C Hydraulic Press RC-53 (USA), fitted with 10-mm round and flat-faced punches.

Release_Properties.Release_Profile: Slowest release among A series; 88.0% DE, 1.42 h MDT.
Release_Properties.Release_Mechanism: Primarily erosion (n=1.11 > 1, indicating super case-II transport).
Release_Properties.Factors_Influencing_Release: Small particle size facilitates rapid gel formation, slowing release.
Medium particle size provides moderate gel formation.
Larger particle size delays gel formation, increasing release rate.

Release_Properties.Release_Profile: Fastest release among A series; 96.4% DE, 0.44 h MDT.
Release_Properties.Release_Mechanism: Primarily diffusion (data too quick to calculate n, indicating burst release).
Release_Properties.Factors_Influencing_Release: Large particle size leads to slow hydration and high porosity, causing burst release.

Release_Properties.Release_Profile: Slowest release among B series; 56.0% DE, 5.20 h MDT.
Release_Properties.Release_Mechanism: Anomalous transport (n=0.82, mixed diffusion and erosion).
Release_Properties.Factors_Influencing_Release: High MW and small particle size create strong, thick gel layer.
Medium particle size with high MW still provides good control.
Larger particles hydrate slower, increasing release rate.

Release_Properties.Release_Profile: Fastest release among B series; 79.0% DE, 2.49 h MDT.
Release_Properties.Factors_Influencing_Release: Large particles hydrate slowly, but high MW still provides some control.

Release_Properties.Release_Profile: Slower release than 003_C4; 80.0% DE, 1.47 h MDT.
Release_Properties.Factors_Influencing_Release: High polymer concentration reduces impact of particle size on release rate.

Release_Properties.Release_Profile: Faster release than 003_C1; 88.0% DE, 1.39 h MDT.
Release_Properties.Factors_Influencing_Release: Release profile similar to 003_C1 (f2 > 50) at this high concentration.

Release_Properties.Release_Profile: Slowest release overall; 41.0% DE, 6.20 h MDT.
Release_Properties.Factors_Influencing_Release: High MW and concentration, and small particle size yield best control.

Release_Properties.Release_Profile: Faster release than 003_D1; 58.0% DE, 5.00 h MDT.
Release_Properties.Factors_Influencing_Release: Particle size effect is significant even at high concentration for high MW PEO (f2 < 50 vs 003_D1).

Experimental_Data.key_results: Smaller PEO particle sizes produce harder tablets with slower drug release. The effect of particle size is more pronounced at lower polymer concentrations (20%) and with higher molecular weight PEO (303).

abstract: Study aim, formulation sets (004_F1–004_F4 polymer ratios; 004_F5–004_F9 SLS levels), dissolution overview, release models, and key conclusion (004_F6 best) [attached_file:1]
introduction: Clinical rationale for sustained release carvedilol, solubility context, and objective of using HPMC matrices with SLS [attached_file:1]
materials_and_methods: Materials origin (vendors), tablet preparation (direct compression), compression device/force/time/punch, blend and tablet tests, UV-Vis calibration (241 nm, absorbance table), dissolution setup (USP II, 0.1 N HCl pH 1.2, 37±0.05°C, 100 rpm, 24 h), release models and equations, MDT equation [attached_file:1]
results_and_discussion: Pre-compression (reported as ranges), tablet properties (reported as ranges), 004_F1–004_F4 and 004_F5–004_F9 dissolution tables and figures, kinetic parameters (004_F5–004_F9), MDT and fractional dissolution times, mechanism interpretation (diffusion + erosion) and selection of 004_F6 [attached_file:1]
conclusion: Summary of successful once-daily sustained-release matrix development and best formulation note [attached_file:1]

materials_origin.api: Carvedilol phosphate (gift sample), Beximco Pharmaceuticals Limited, Bangladesh [attached_file:1]
excipients: Methocel K4M CR, Methocel K15M CR, Avicel PH101 (Dow Chemical’s Asia Pvt., India) [attached_file:1]
Sodium lauryl sulfate (Sigma-Aldrich Chemie GmbH, Germany) [attached_file:1]
materials_origin.solvents_and_grade: Reagent-grade chemicals; distilled water used throughout [attached_file:1]

experimental_design_for_optimization.overview: Two-stage optimization: 004_F1–004_F4 varied HPMC K4M/K15M ratio to sustain 24 h; 004_F5–004_F9 fixed polymer ratio and varied SLS (1.0–2.0%) to enhance dissolution of poorly soluble carvedilol [attached_file:1]

sets.name: 004_F1–004_F4
sets.factor: HPMC K15M level while K4M fixed (matrix polymer ratio) [attached_file:1]
sets.objective: Optimize polymer composition to avoid burst and sustain release up to 24 h [attached_file:1]

sets.name: 004_F5–004_F9
sets.factor: SLS percentage with fixed polymers (K4M 45 mg, K15M 33 mg) [attached_file:1]
sets.objective: Increase dissolution rate due to poor aqueous solubility while matching theoretical once-daily profile [attached_file:1]

ingredients_identification_uv_vis.how_prepared: 10 mg carvedilol in 50 mL flask; add 1 mL methanol, sonicate 5 min; cool; make to volume with distilled water; filter (Whatman 42 then 0.45 μm); prepare 1–8 μg/mL standards [attached_file:1]
ingredients_identification_uv_vis.instrument_and_wavelength: Double-beam UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) at 241 nm [attached_file:1]
equations_used_in_methods: Korsmeyer–Peppas: \( \frac{M_t}{M_\alpha} = k t^{n} \) with mechanism by n [attached_file:1]

pre_compression_parameters.reported_as: ranges across blends (not itemized per batch)

tablet_fabrication_quality_testing.fabrication_method: Direct compression after dry blending; blend sieved #24; lubricant and glidant added last; compressed with 10 mm flat-faced punch [attached_file:1]
qc_tests_and_equipment: Weight variation (20 tablets) using Sartorius electronic balance [attached_file:1]
Hardness and thickness via Dr. Schleuniger Pharmatron Tablet Tester (Model 6D) [attached_file:1]
tablet_fabrication_quality_testing.per_batch_metrics: Not itemized per batch (004_F1–004_F9) for hardness, friability, thickness, diameter, density, drug content, or consolidation index in the paper [attached_file:1]

process_parameters.post_compression_storage: Airtight polyethylene bags at room temperature [attached_file:1]

dissolution_conditions.medium: 900 mL simulated gastric fluid (0.1 N HCl, pH 1.2) [attached_file:1]
dissolution_conditions.assay: UV-Vis at 241 nm using calibration curve [attached_file:1]

mechanism_interpretation.F5_to_F9: Anomalous transport (coupled diffusion and erosion) based on n = 0.628–0.655 and good linearity in Korsmeyer–Peppas and Higuchi models [attached_file:1]
mechanism_interpretation.F1_to_F4: Not optimized for target profile; mechanism not reported [attached_file:1]

release_properties.best_formulation: 004_F6 (meets theoretical profile with lower SLS compared to 004_F7–004_F9) [attached_file:1]

per_batch_F1_to_F9.note: Hardness, friability, weight variation, thickness, diameter, density, drug content, consolidation index not itemized per batch in the paper; overall ranges reported under QC [attached_file:1]

swelling_or_water_uptake.note: No swelling or floating studies reported [attached_file:1]

experimental_data_summary.in_vitro: Dissolution in 0.1 N HCl pH 1.2, 24 h, USP II, with zero- and first-order, Higuchi, Korsmeyer–Peppas, and Hixson–Crowell analyses; 004_F6–004_F9 satisfied theoretical profile; 004_F6 selected [attached_file:1]
experimental_data_summary.statistics: Model fits reported via R²; MDT computed per Mockel and Lippold; no inferential statistics beyond regression fits [attached_file:1]

article_metadata.title: Bilayer tablets with sustained‑release metformin and immediate‑release sitagliptin: preparation and in vitro/in vivo evaluation

section_index.abstract: Purpose, methods overview, headline in vitro/in vivo results, conclusion [attached_file:1]
section_index.introduction: Clinical context, rationale, limitations of coating, prior reports [attached_file:1]
section_index.materials_and_methods: Materials origin, preparation of each layer, bilayer compression, dissolution/HPLC, LC–MS/MS, DoE [attached_file:1]
section_index.results_and_discussion: Optimization (DoE), tablet quality, stability, release profiles and f2, excipient effects [attached_file:1]

standards.metformin_standard: Institute of Drug Quality Control Ho Chi Minh City (92.20%) [attached_file:1]
standards.phenformin_internal_standard: Institute of Drug Quality Control Ho Chi Minh City (95.00%) [attached_file:1]

materials_origin.grades: Other excipients ≥ analytical grade; HPLC solvents chromatographic grade [attached_file:1]

experimental_design_for_optimization.selection_criterion: Similarity to Janumet XR dissolution (f2 > 50) and acceptable tablet properties [attached_file:1]

dissolution_assay_detection.mobile_phase: 5 mM sodium laureth sulfate with 0.1% triethylamine (pH 4.0 with phosphoric acid):methanol 30:70 v/v [attached_file:1]

bioanalytical_quantification.mobile_phase: 0.1% formic acid:methanol 45:55 v/v, 1.0 mL/min, 10 µL injection [attached_file:1]

ingredients_identification.notes: Absorbance per se not reported; quantification based on HPLC-UV area and LC–MS/MS MRM [attached_file:1]

formulation_details_per_layer_optimal_ba.notes: Table 1 lists only the optimal composition; other DoE trial compositions are in Supplementary Data [attached_file:1]

metformin_layer.notes: Not reported separately for individual layers [attached_file:1]

sitagliptin_layer.notes: Not reported separately for individual layers [attached_file:1]

metformin_layer.notes: Measurements reported for final bilayer only [attached_file:1]

sitagliptin_layer.notes: Measurements reported for final bilayer only [attached_file:1]

wet_granulation_metformin_layer.sieving: 0.3 mm pre-sieve of powders; wet mass pressed through 0.8 mm screen [attached_file:1]
wet_granulation_metformin_layer.post_dry_blend: Sieving 0.8 mm, then blend with carbopol, magnesium stearate, Aerosil [attached_file:1]

direct_compression_sitagliptin_layer.sieving: SG and sodium starch glycolate through 0.3 mm [attached_file:1]
direct_compression_sitagliptin_layer.blending: With Ludipress, A‑tab, PVP K90 in cube mixer; add magnesium stearate and Aerosil [attached_file:1]

bilayer_compression.first_compression: Metformin layer to intermediate tablet at 7 ± 2 kg/cm² [attached_file:1]
bilayer_compression.final_compression: Add SG layer and compress at 18 ± 3 kg/cm² [attached_file:1]

dissolution_test.HPLC_detection: UV 265 nm with conditions as above [attached_file:1]

bioanalytical_LC_MS_MS.instrument: Triple Quadrupole Mass Spectrometer Xevo (ESI+, MRM) [attached_file:1]

metformin_sustained_release.mechanistic_factors: HPMC K100M slight pre-swelling via limited water in granulation traps MH; carbopol forms outer gel retarding early release [attached_file:1]

excipient_effects_at_pH_6_8.interpretation: Carbopol further sustains MH release, notably at 1–2 h [attached_file:1]

bilayer_tablet.stability: Physicochemically stable at room temperature for ≥1 year [attached_file:1]
bilayer_tablet.notes: Disintegration time and swelling/floating not applicable/reported [attached_file:1]

in_vivo_bioequivalence.design: Open-label, randomized, single-dose, two-period crossover; n=14 healthy adults; fasting; 14-day washout [attached_file:1]

additional_notes: The bilayer approach avoids solvent/heat steps of SG coating, reducing risk of polymorphic change and oxidative degradation [attached_file:1]
No FTIR, swelling/floating, or classical kinetic model fitting reported [attached_file:1]

contains: Study aim, overview of three formulations (F-1: 40% guar gum, F-2: 50% guar gum, F-3: control without guar), 50 mg curcumin per tablet, wet granulation method, evaluation metrics (hardness, friability, content uniformity), in vitro mouth-to-colon release design, and key result that F-1 released 91.1% at 24 h with rat caecal contents vs 82.1% for F-2
abstract.notes: Summarizes formulation strategy and comparative release outcomes in simulated GI conditions with/without rat caecal contents

contains: Rationale for colon-targeted delivery, advantages of colon as a site, curcumin background and therapeutic relevance, and justification for using degradable polysaccharides such as guar gum for colon targeting
introduction.notes: Frames need for matrix systems that protect in stomach/small intestine and release in colon

contains: Chemicals and sources (guar gum and curcumin), characterization of guar gum (particle traits, angle of repose, densities, pH, ash, LOD, solubility), and formulation components/percentages (Table 2)
materials.notes: Vendor information and excipient profiles; guar gum quality parameters in Table 1

contains: Wet granulation process steps, sieving sizes, drying conditions, tableting equipment, evaluation tests (weight variation, hardness, friability, content uniformity), dissolution media sequence, USP Apparatus 1 settings, UV analysis at 421 nm, and rat caecal content preparation for microbiota-susceptibility testing
methods.notes: Full preparation and test conditions to replicate in vitro mouth-to-colon release and microbiota-mediated degradation

contains: Physical evaluation (weight, hardness, friability, content uniformity in Table 3), dissolution profiles without rat caecal contents (Table 4), dissolution profiles with rat caecal contents (Table 5), and interpretation that guar gum protects in upper GI and enhances colonic release via enzymatic degradation
results_and_discussion.notes: Comparative performance across F-1/F-2/F-3 and effect of rat caecal enzymes on release

contains: Guar gum matrices protect curcumin in stomach/small intestine and enable colonic release, potentially reducing dose while increasing bioavailability for colon cancer therapy
conclusion.notes: Positions guar gum as a promising carrier for colon-specific curcumin delivery

actives_and_excipients: Curcumin and guar gum purchased from National Chemical, Vadodara, Gujarat, India

guar_gum_characterization.particle_characters: White to off-white powder, nearly neutral flavor, odor, color
guar_gum_characterization.solubility: Soluble in hot and cold water

experimental_design_for_optimization.design_type: Comparative formulations with varying guar gum levels (no formal DOE or optimization model reported)
formulations_compared: F-1: 40% guar gum matrix tablets with 10% curcumin
F-2: 50% guar gum matrix tablets with 10% curcumin
F-3: Control without guar gum (85% lactose) with 10% curcumin
experimental_design_for_optimization.primary_endpoint: Cumulative curcumin release over time across GI-mimicking media with and without rat caecal contents

how_prepared: Curcumin, guar gum (<250 µm), and lactose (<250 µm) were sieved; drug was sieved <150 µm; powders blended and wet granulated with 8% starch paste; wet mass passed through sieve #18, dried at 50°C for 2 h, sized through #22, lubricated with talc:Mg stearate (2:1), and compressed on eight-station tabletting machine

analytical_details.absorbance_notes: Quantified by UV method at 421 nm using standard curves in each dissolution medium; pre-filtering not applied prior to sampling

results_summary: F-1 and F-2 minimized release in upper GI media and showed enhanced release with rat caecal contents, indicating enzymatic degradation of guar gum in colonic conditions

composition_percent_w_w.Starch paste (8%): q.s. for granulation (binder solution concentration; not part of % w/w totals)

F-1.notes: Percentages sum to 100% excluding binder solution; per abstract each tablet contains ~50 mg curcumin

composition_percent_w_w.Starch paste (8%): q.s. for granulation (binder solution concentration; not part of % w/w totals)

F-2.notes: Higher guar gum content reduces release in upper GI and modestly reduces total 24 h release compared to F-1

composition_percent_w_w.Starch paste (8%): q.s. for granulation (binder solution concentration; not part of % w/w totals)

F-3.notes: Control tablets without guar gum show rapid release in upper GI media (63.2% by 5 h)

F-1.granule_flow: Not reported (only guar gum powder properties reported)
F-1.hardness: Not applicable pre-compression; see tablet properties
F-1.friability: Not applicable pre-compression; see tablet properties
F-1.size: Sieved: drug <150 µm; guar gum and lactose <250 µm; granules sized via #18 then #22

F-2.granule_flow: Not reported (only guar gum powder properties reported)
F-2.hardness: Not applicable pre-compression; see tablet properties
F-2.friability: Not applicable pre-compression; see tablet properties
F-2.size: Sieved: drug <150 µm; guar gum and lactose <250 µm; granules sized via #18 then #22

F-3.granule_flow: Not reported (only guar gum powder properties reported)
F-3.hardness: Not applicable pre-compression; see tablet properties
F-3.friability: Not applicable pre-compression; see tablet properties
F-3.size: Sieved: drug <150 µm; lactose <250 µm; granules sized via #18 then #22

controlled_factors: Curcumin fixed at 10% w/w per tablet (~50 mg)
SLS fixed at 2% w/w as solubilizer
Magnesium stearate 1% w/w and talc 2% w/w as lubricant/glidant
Lactose q.s. to balance formulation (35–85% depending on batch)
dissolution_media_sequence: 0.1N HCl (900 mL) for 2 h at 37°C, 100 rpm (USP Apparatus 1)
pH 7.4 phosphate buffer (900 mL) for 3 h at 37°C, 100 rpm
pH 6.8 phosphate buffer (900 mL) up to 24 h at 37°C, 100 rpm

006_Colon targeted.ftir_measurements: Not reported; no FTIR analysis described in this study

measurement_methods: Evaluation per standard tablet tests: weight variation, hardness, friability, and content uniformity

granulation: Wet granulation with 8% w/w starch paste as binder; powders blended then granulated
sieving_and_sizing: Curcumin <150 µm; guar gum and lactose <250 µm before blending; wet mass passed through sieve #18; dried granules re-sized through sieve #22
lubrication: Talc–magnesium stearate mixture (2:1) used as lubricant prior to compression
compression: Eight-station tabletting machine; compression pressure not reported; no coating applied
dissolution_testing: USP Apparatus 1 (basket), 100 rpm, 37°C; media sequence: 0.1N HCl 2 h, pH 7.4 buffer 3 h, pH 6.8 buffer to 24 h; 900 mL each stage
analysis: UV spectrophotometry at 421 nm; samples 5 mL withdrawn at intervals, replaced with fresh buffer; analyzed using standard curves per medium
rat_caecal_preparation: Male albino rats pre-dosed with 1% guar gum dispersion (4 mL/day) for 7 days; caecal contents harvested, 4% w/v in pH 6.8 buffer, 006_CO2 bubbled; used to simulate colonic enzymatic environment

influencing_factors: Minimal release in acidic (pH ~1.2) and upper GI buffer stages; matrix integrity attributed to guar gum gel formation
Higher guar gum content further retards release in upper GI media
Absence of guar gum leads to rapid release in upper GI conditions
Enhanced release due to enzymatic degradation of guar gum by caecal microflora
Enzymatic susceptibility of guar gum increases release in simulated colonic environment

release_kinetics.mechanism_interpretation: Qualitative: diffusion/erosion via guar gum degradation in colonic conditions; no model fitting reported
Qualitative: higher polymer fraction increases matrix integrity and slows release; no model fitting reported
Rapid dissolution due to absence of matrix-forming polymer; no model fitting reported

in_vitro: Dissolution profiles in staged GI media (0.1N HCl → pH 7.4 → pH 6.8) without rat caecal contents for F-1/F-2/F-3 (Table 4)
Dissolution profiles in pH 6.8 buffer with 4% w/v rat caecal contents for F-1/F-2 (Table 5)
experimental_data.statistics: Not reported (no SD/SE or model fits shown for dissolution kinetics)
performance_metrics: 24 h cumulative release without caecal contents: F-1 68.7%, F-2 55.7%
24 h cumulative release with caecal contents: F-1 91.1%, F-2 82.1%

notes: No FTIR characterization, kinetic modeling, disintegration time, stability studies, thickness/diameter, formulation density, SD for tests, or compression force were reported in this article

paper_metadata.title: Combining Liquisolid and Co‑grinding Techniques to Enhance the Dissolution Rate of Celecoxib

sections_likely_locations.abstract: First page under title and author list
sections_likely_locations.introduction: Starts after abstract; context on dissolution enhancement and celecoxib BCS II characteristics
sections_likely_locations.materials: Section titled Materials listing API and excipient sources and grades
sections_likely_locations.methods_preparation: Preparation of celecoxib formulations: combination (liquisolid + ball mill), conventional liquisolid, direct compression
sections_likely_locations.methods_tests: Dissolution testing (USP II, media, sampling, UV quantification), friability, PXRD, DSC, aging study
sections_likely_locations.results_discussion: Dissolution comparisons in SGF vs SIF and across LSB/LS/DC, PXRD, DSC, aging effects with figures and discussion
sections_likely_locations.conclusion: Final section summarizing enhanced dissolution and stability outcomes

carriers_excipients: Microcrystalline cellulose Avicel PH101 (Mingtai Chemical, Taiwan)
media_reagents: Sodium chloride, KH2PO4, NaOH, HCl 37% (Merck, Germany) for SGF and SIF preparation

formulation_families: LSB (Liquisolid + Ball milling; co‑ground) with API in liquid medication at 30%, 40%, 50%: 007_LSB1, 007_LSB2, 007_LSB3
DC (Direct compression/physical mixture) at nominal 30%, 40%, 50% API load series: 007_DC1, 007_DC2, 007_DC3

compression_force: 150 kg/cm^2 (LSB and LS used to reach 5–7 kg/cm^2 hardness)
aging_condition: 6 months at 25 °C, protected from moisture and light (tested on 40% LSB)

responses: Cumulative % drug release vs time (up to 120 min)
Solid‑state state (PXRD peaks and intensities; DSC melting endotherm)

preparation_summary.LSB: Celecoxib dispersed in PEG 200; milled in planetary ball mill (PM 100 Retsch, 50‑mL zirconia chamber, stainless steel balls 5–10 cm) for 3 h; PVP added and mixed 5 min; Avicel added per load factor at R=20 (carrier:coating); silica (1:20 to Avicel) sieved 60‑mesh added; sodium starch glycolate 3.5% w/w added and mixed 15 min; lubricated with magnesium stearate; tablets with 100 mg celecoxib compressed to 5–7 kg/cm^2 using 50/100/150 kg/cm^2 on Riken P‑16B
preparation_summary.LS: Same as LSB but without ball milling; celecoxib mixed with PEG 200 by mortar/pestle to uniform dispersion; same excipient ratios and compression to hardness 5–7 kg/cm^2
preparation_summary.DC: Same as liquisolid excipient base but without non‑volatile co‑solvent; compressed with 12 mm flat‑faced punch at 50 kg/cm^2

quantification.dissolution_sampling: USP II, 900 mL media, 50 rpm, 37 °C; sampling at 5, 10, 15, 30, 45, 60, 90, 120 min; replace 5 mL; 0.45 µm filter; UV–vis at 249 nm; calibration based on standard curve
quantification.correction_equation: Sampling correction: C_tn = C_on + (v/V) Σ_{i=1}^{n−1} C_oi where v=sample volume, V=total medium volume
quantification.profile_similarity: Similarity factor f2 = 50 log{[1 + (1/n) Σ_t (R_t − T_t)^2]^{−0.5} × 100}; f2 < 50 indicates significant difference
quantification.absorbance_wavelength: UV–vis quantification at 249 nm for celecoxib

pre_compression_parameters_per_batch.notes: Mass distribution, hardness SD, friability SD, and size SD per batch were not fully tabulated; friability raw 007_W1/007_W2 listed in Table 4; hardness target 5–7 kg/cm^2 for LSB/LS; no batch‑wise SDs reported for hardness or size; DC compressed at fixed 50 kg/cm^2 with 12 mm punch

fourier_transform_infrared_spectral_meas.note: FTIR not performed/reported; solid‑state analysis used PXRD and DSC; no FTIR section or spectra mentioned

process.compression_device: Manual tableting machine Riken P‑16B (Tokyo, Japan) for LS/LSB; 12 mm flat‑faced punch for DC
process.compression_force: LS/LSB: 50, 100, 150 kg/cm^2 to achieve hardness 5–7 kg/cm^2; DC: 50 kg/cm^2
process.target_hardness: 5–7 kg/cm^2; aging showed 6–7 kg/cm^2 after 6 months for 40% LSB

per_batch_QC.hardness: Reported as target range; specific per‑batch mean±SD not tabulated; aged 40% LSB unchanged at 6–7 kg/cm^2
per_batch_QC.weight_variation: Not explicitly tabulated per batch; tablet weights per formulation provided in Tables 1–3; no SDs reported
per_batch_QC.thickness_diameter: Diameter 12 mm for DC; thickness not reported; LS/LSB punch dimensions not specified beyond compression device; no SDs reported
per_batch_QC.drug_content: Not reported; quantification focused on dissolution UV assay

liquisolid_preparation.R_ratio: Carrier to coating R=20 (Avicel:silica 20:1 by weight)
liquisolid_preparation.mixing_times: PVP to liquid medication 5 min; final blend with SSG 15 min

co_grinding.equipment: Planetary ball mill PM 100 Retsch with 50‑mL zirconia chamber; stainless steel balls 5–10 cm

compression.device: Riken P‑16B manual press; DC also used 12 mm flat‑faced punch
compression.pressure: 50/100/150 kg/cm^2 for LS/LSB; 50 kg/cm^2 for DC

process_parameters.coating: No tablet film coating applied; ‘coating’ refers to silica powder coating in liquisolid powder

release_properties_per_batch.test_conditions: USP II, 900 mL SGF pH 1 and SIF pH 7.2, 50 rpm, 37 °C; UV at 249 nm; non‑sink conditions acknowledged

mechanism_models.reported_models: Profiles compared with f2; narrative references Noyes–Whitney and diffusion boundary layer theory; no formal zero/first order, Higuchi, Hixson‑Crowell, or Korsmeyer–Peppas model fitting parameters reported per batch

profiles_and_findings.007_LSB1: In SIF and SGF, cumulative release after 120 min approximately 28.53% (SIF) and 18.1% (SGF); no significant difference between SIF and SGF (pH effect minimal due to celecoxib pKa 11.1)
profiles_and_findings.LSB_series_vs_LS_vs_DC: For 30%, 40%, 50% series in SIF, LSB showed fastest release > LS > DC; at 60 min LSB approx 7.5× DC; increased API% in liquid medication reduced release rate (30% > 40% > 50%)
profiles_and_findings.aging_effect: 40% LSB aged 6 months showed f2 > 50 vs fresh in both SIF and SGF indicating similar profiles; no leaks or cracks observed

factors_influencing_release: Hydrophilic liquid vehicle improves wettability and specific surface area, enhancing dissolution
Co‑grinding reduces particle size and increases specific surface area; potential local co‑solvency increases Cs in boundary layer per Noyes–Whitney
Higher API fraction in liquid medication reduces degree of molecular dispersion, lowering release rate
Bulk pH between 1 and 7.2 shows limited effect due to celecoxib pKa 11.1 under non‑sink conditions

hardness.DC: Not specified beyond compression at 50 kg/cm^2; hardness values not tabulated

friability_percent.per_batch: See Table 4 values; all below 1% acceptance

dissolution_profile.summary: Figures 2–4 show LSB > LS > DC across 30/40/50% in SIF; 007_LSB1 detailed in Fig. 1 for SGF vs SIF

stability.aging: No visual leakage or structural change; hardness and dissolution unchanged (f2 > 50) over 6 months at 25 °C for 40% LSB

tablet_properties_per_batch.weight_variation_percent: Not reported; only batch total tablet weight given per formulation

in_vitro.dissolution: Time points 5–120 min; UV at 249 nm; correction applied; LSB showed significantly faster dissolution vs DC and LS; non‑sink acknowledged
in_vitro.friability: Per‑batch 007_W1, 007_W2, friability% in Table 4; all within <1%
in_vitro.PXRD: Celecoxib sharp peaks at 2θ ≈ 5°, 10.5°, 16°, 21.5°; LS and DC similar patterns with reduced peak intensity suggesting lower crystallinity; no new peaks indicating no interaction with excipients
in_vitro.DSC: Pure celecoxib melting endotherm at 161.26 °C; small peak in physical mixture from low drug fraction; no melting peak in co‑ground and classic liquisolid indicating reduced crystallinity/amorphousness
in_vitro.aging: Hardness stable (6–7 kg/cm^2) and dissolution f2 > 50 after 6 months for 40% LSB

experimental_data.in_vivo: No in vivo studies reported in this article
experimental_data.release_kinetics_fitting: No batch‑wise zero/first order, Higuchi, Hixson‑Crowell, or Korsmeyer–Peppas parameters reported; only mechanistic discussion and f2 comparison provided

equations.mechanistic_reference: Noyes–Whitney dissolution context used qualitatively; no numerical fitting presented

unreported_items: Pre‑compression flow properties, mass distribution SD, hardness SD per batch, thickness, diameter (for LSB/LS), density, assay/drug content, consolidation index, disintegration time, tablet swelling metrics, and formal kinetic model fits are not reported

experimental_design_for_optimization.aim: Prepare extended-release matrix pellets by extrusion-spheronization using different polymers, compress into tablets, optimize for release and uniformity
methods: Dry blend mixed in Rapid mixer granulator Allen Bradley 5L, USA
Extrusion (Multi gran MG 55, Fugi Paudal Co Ltd Japan) with 0.8 mm screen
Spheronization (QJ 230T-1, Fugi Paudal Co Ltd Japan, crosshatch plate 1mm, 850 rpm, 4-15 min)
Fluid bed drying (Retsch, Germany, inlet air 50°C, 20-30 min)
Pellet fraction: ASTM 20 mesh (841 µm), fines below 40 mesh (420 µm) discarded
Calcium ion complexation for alginate batches: Wet pellets poured into CaCl₂ solutions (10%, 20%, saturated), stirred 10 min, filtered

ingredients_identification.polymers: Aquacoat ECD, Eudragit L 30D 55, Eudragit NM 30D, Sodium Alginate; different batches use different types and concentrations
ingredients_identification.preparation_summary: Tablet batches 008_TAB1-008_TAB5 use calcium chloride-treated pellets, microcrystalline cellulose, polyethylene glycol, crospovidone, magnesium stearate

008_Compression-and-evaluation-of-extend.Fourier_Transform_Infrared_Measurements_: Not explicitly reported; SEM used for surface characterization instead.

tablet_fabrications.device: 12-station automated single rotary compression machine (Smart Press, SRC 10i, Pacific Industries, India), 19.5 x 9.5 mm capsule shaped biconvex tooling, speed 10 rpm

process_parameters.drying: Fluid bed dryer inlet air 50°C, 20-30 min

pellets.release_kinetics: Not specified as zero/first order for all, but extended release evident. Saturated CaCl₂ gives slowest release.

tablets.compression force: No significant effect on release profile, tablets rapidly disintegrate into pellets

tablet_properties.dissolution_profile: Similar for both pellets and tablets (see figures)
tablet_properties.stability: No significant change after 3 month (99.2% drug retained)
tablet_properties.weight_variation_percent: Not explicitly stated, but Acceptance Value (AV) used for content uniformity (008_TAB1: 18.7, 008_TAB2: 12.2, 008_TAB3: 14)

experimental_data.release_profiles: See figures: Zero/first order release not separately detailed

experimental_data.in_vitro_release_profiles: Provided for all batches, see Figures 1A, 1B, 1C, 1D, 2, 4, 7, 8, 9, 10
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: SD used for AV calculation, values for each batch not detailed

article_sections.abstract: Summary of aims, design, buoyancy, dissolution, and in vivo findings.
article_sections.introduction: Rationale for MT-1207 GRDDS and polymer choices.
materials_and_methods: Blend flowability (bulk/tapped density, Carr index, Hausner ratio)
In vivo Beagle PK and bioanalytical method
article_sections.results_and_discussion: Flowability, factorial outcomes, optimization, mechanical properties, buoyancy vs pH, in vitro release, kinetics, PK.
article_sections.conclusion: Viable once-daily gastroretentive bilayer tablets with immediate and prolonged buoyancy.

materials_origin.material: MT-1207 HCl reference standard and 5:1 micronized MT-1207:colloidal silicon dioxide

Polyethylene oxide 8,000,000 (PEO 8M), Kollidon SR, spray-dried lactose, PVP K30, HPMC K15M, HPMC K100M, colloidal silicon dioxide, magnesium stearate, sodium phosphate monobasic dihydrate, sodium acetate anhydrous
Alpha-D-lactose monohydrate, ethylcellulose 10 cps, iron(III) oxide, HCl 37%, acetic acid, NaOH pellets, orthophosphoric acid, methanol (HPLC), propan-2-ol (HPLC)

experimental_design_for_optimization.design: Face-centered central composite design for gastroretentive layer (27 runs).

modeling.FLT_model_equation: Provided as Eq. 7 with quadratic terms; R^2 = 0.829; adjusted R^2 = 0.738
modeling.TFT_model_equation: Provided as Eq. 8 (and reduced Eq. 9 without non-significant linear 009_X3); R^2 ≈ 0.923; adjusted R^2 ≈ 0.883–0.887

key_effects_summary: FLT increased with compression force; interaction PEO×force negative at 7.5 kN; choose ~7–7.5 kN to balance FLT, hardness, wet strength.
TFT increased with PEO (linear and quadratic), non-linear effect of compression force; interaction PEO×force negative at higher PEO.
Kollidon SR level (fixed at ~34% w/w in gastro layer) boosted wet strength more than buoyancy.

analytical_method_HPLC_UV.mobile_phase: 0.1 M sodium acetate buffer pH 4.0 : methanol = 40:60 v/v
analytical_method_HPLC_UV.validation: ICH/FDA for linearity, LOD/LOQ; specificity, accuracy, precision aligned with dissolution validation

equations_used: General polynomial (Eq. 1) linking Y to 009_X1, 009_X2, 009_X3 and interactions/quadratics for factorial design.
First-order release: Log(009_Q1) = Log(009_Q0) − k1 t / 2.303 (Eq. 2).

pre_compression_parameters.blend_flowability: Carr index and Hausner ratio indicated passable to good flowability for both gastro and drug-layer blends (see Supplementary Tables 009_S1–S2).
pre_compression_parameters.per_batch_values: Not numerically reported in main text; refer to Supplementary data.
pre_compression_parameters.mass_distribution_weight_variation: All bilayer batches had acceptable mean weight and USP <905> acceptance value < 15 (see Supplementary Table 009_S5).
pre_compression_parameters.hardness_friability: Friability < 1.0% for all bilayer tablets; hardness/tensile strength sufficient for mechanical integrity (see Supplementary Table 009_S4).
pre_compression_parameters.dimensions: Optimised and 009_A1 swollen dimensions after buoyancy: long axis ≈ 25.8–26.1 mm, short axis ≈ 13.5–13.7 mm, thickness ≈ 12.9–13.2 mm (n=3).

fabrication.gastro_layer: Direct blending: PEO, Kollidon SR, SDL, HPMC K100M, colloidal silica mixed 15 min; add Mg stearate and mix 5 min.
fabrication.drug_layer: Wet granulation per prior 009_A1 procedure; composition varies by batch.
fabrication.compression: Piccola rotary press (Riva), two-step: pre-compress gastro layer, add drug layer and compress; forces: 5, 7, 7.5, or 10 kN; tooling: 13 mm flat round and 17×9 mm concave oblong, depending on tablet weight.

quality_tests.hardness: USP <1217> on 009_C50 tester; tensile strength calculated.
quality_tests.density_porosity: Helium pycnometry and mercury intrusion porosimetry used on optimized tablets.

tablet_fabrication_and_testing.drug_content_assay: Quantified via validated HPLC-UV method in dissolution samples; tablet drug content not separately reported.
tablet_fabrication_and_testing.consolidation_index: Not reported; compressibility summarized by Carr index/Hausner ratio in Supplementary.

compression_process.pre_compression: Applied to gastro layer before adding drug layer
compression_process.notes: Lower force favored FLT but higher force improved hardness and wet strength; ~7–7.5 kN selected.

testing_equipment_conditions.apparatus: USP II, 75 rpm (also 200 rpm), 500 mL 0.1 M acetate pH 4.0, 37±0.5 °C

testing_equipment_conditions.media: FaSSGF pH 1.6 for screening; HCl pH 1.0, pH 3.0, pH 6.0 for pH effect, 75 rpm, 37±0.5 °C

testing_equipment_conditions.instrument: Philips 009_XL30; 10 kV; samples dried at 40 °C, gold-sputtered

dissolution_method.apparatus: USP II, 75 rpm; 500 mL 0.1 M acetate buffer pH 4.0; 37±0.5 °C; sampling at 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 16, 24 h; centrifuge then HPLC-UV
dissolution_method.sinkers: Stainless-steel sinkers used to negate buoyancy effect
dissolution_method.high_shear_assessment: 200 rpm study for FHE6005 and 009_A1; profiles similar to 75 rpm (f2 > 50)

per_batch_summary_drug_layer.note: In vitro release similar to bilayer 009_A1 (f1=13, f2=59)
No distinct numerical profile reported in main text
No distinct numerical profile reported in main text
No distinct numerical profile reported in main text
No distinct numerical profile reported in main text
Only one of two batches (with FHE6005) achieving ≥80% mean release by 24 h
No distinct numerical profile reported in main text
All three tablets ≥80% released by 24 h; selected as optimized ethylcellulose-containing drug layer
No distinct numerical profile reported in main text
No distinct numerical profile reported in main text

profile_comparisons: Single-layer 009_A1 vs bilayer 009_A1: f1=40, f2=36; bilayer slows release vs single-layer.
Bilayer 009_A1 vs bilayer FHE7035: f1=13, f2=59; similar profiles.
FHE6005 009_A1 at 200 rpm vs 75 rpm: f2 = 89 and 76 respectively (similar).
factors_influencing_release: Ethylcellulose level had minor effect in vitro; gastro layer presence dominated by reducing exposed surface area of drug layer.
At pH 1.0, 3.0, 6.0 buoyancy duration varied, potentially via acid-induced PEO degradation affecting floatation and tablet integrity; release testing itself used pH 4.0 acetate.

mechanical_properties_summary: Hardness/tensile strength sufficient for integrity across batches; no clear trend with ethylcellulose level (values in Supplementary).
Weight variation within USP limits; acceptance value < 15 for all.

density_porosity_optimized.interpretation: Bulk density < 1.004 g/cm3 consistent with near-immediate floatation.

experimental_data_additional.SEM_summary: Dry gastro layer: porous surface with irregular PEO and spherical Kollidon SR particles; at 12 h fibers indicative of swollen/dried PEO; fibers decrease at 22 h in pH 1.0; persistent to 31–43.5 h in pH 6.0.
experimental_data_additional.viscosity_summary: No inter-media differences up to 12 h; significant divergence by 22 h consistent with PEO degradation at low pH; stable between 31 and 43.5 h at pH 6.0.

in_vivo_PKuctions.design: 4 male Beagle dogs (10 ± 2 kg), fed state; dosing: 30 mg bilayer FHE6005 o.d., 30 mg bilayer 009_A1 o.d., and three 10 mg IR tablets; serial sampling up to 32 h; 1-week washout; UPLC-MS/MS quantitation.
in_vivo_PKuctions.outcome_summary: 24 h sustained plasma levels above ~1 ng/mL threshold; both bilayer formulations demonstrated 24 h release in vivo; Beagle half-life shorter than human, supporting feasibility for once-daily in humans.

notes_missing_or_not_applicable: Disintegration time and swelling index (%): not reported.
Per-batch hardness, friability, and weight variation values: referenced to Supplementary Tables 009_S4–009_S5, not numerically listed in main text.
Pre-compression Carr index/Hausner ratio per blend: referenced to Supplementary Tables 009_S1–S2, not numerically listed in main text.
Percent of pellets: not applicable to this tablet system.

source.article_title: Core in Cup Ethylmorphine Hydrochloride Tablet for Dual Fast and Sustained Pain Relief: Formulation, Characterization, and Pharmacokinetic Study

materials_origin.Ethylmorphine_hydrochloride: gift sample from Macfarlan Smith Ltd., Edinburgh (UK).
materials_origin.Methyl_cellulose: Dow Chemical Company, Midland, MI, USA.

experimental_design_for_optimization.selection_criteria: From ODT factorial results, two formulas (010_F9 & 010_F10) selected as cup (fast IR). From SR factorial 010_S6 selected as sustained core based on in vitro release profile. These choices are stated in the article.

ODT_lyophilization.binder_hydration: Gelatin or methyl cellulose dispersed in 0.2 mL distilled water for 30 min then 2 mL added; heated to 40°C until clear.
ODT_lyophilization.add_excipients: Mannitol or maltodextrin and glycine (20 mg) added and dissolved; EtM added; aliquots of 2 mL placed into blister pockets (2 cm diameter), frozen at -20°C for 24 h.
ODT_lyophilization.lyophilizer: Christ Alpha 1-2LD plus (Munich, Germany). Condenser temp -55°C, vacuum 7.6 Pa. Primary drying 12 h at -30°C and 0.37 mbar; secondary drying 12 h at 20°C and 0.01 mbar.

SR_direct_compression.equipment: Single punch tablet machine (Shakti Pharmatech, Model SSTP-12, India) with flat-faced 8-mm punch/die.
SR_direct_compression.procedure: Powder components fed manually into die and compressed with constant compression force; each tablet final weight 150 mg containing 40 mg EtM (for SR formulas as composed).

ingredients_identification_notes.glycine: collapse protectant (20 mg in ODT formulas)

UV_spectrophotometry.use_case: Drug content assay for tablets (dissolved in phosphate buffer pH 6.8).

HPLC_for_in_vivo_samples.mobile_phase: water (pH 5.4 with orthophosphoric acid) : acetonitrile 40:60 v/v
HPLC_for_in_vivo_samples.instrument: Shimadzu LC-10ADvp with SPD-20A UV/VIS detector

formulation_details_per_batch.notes: All batch composition values below are taken directly from Table II of the article. Where table text was structured in two parts (Cup and Core) the aggregated composition of final core-in-cup (010_B1, 010_B2) is provided in the 010_B1/010_B2 entries. For 010_S1–010_S9 the SR tablet ingredient masses are shown as given.

010_S2.note_on_table_parsing: Table text for 010_S2 in the PDF is compacted; the article table shows EtM=45 mg and HPMC=52 mg—other columns are blank in the printed table.

010_S3.note_on_table_parsing: 010_S3 row in the article table: EtM=60 mg and HPMC=37 mg.

010_S4.note_on_table_parsing: 010_S4 row layout indicates MC=30, Mg stearate=3, KCl=10, lactose=67; EtM value not explicitly printed in that row in the compact PDF excerpt—interpretation: some rows were split in two-line format in the table; see Article text for explicit dosing logic (total core dose usually 40 mg EtM).

010_S5.note_on_table_parsing: As above, table formatting in PDF compresses entries; taken as presented in Table II.

010_S6.note_on_selection: 010_S6 is explicitly stated in the article as the selected SR formula (best sustained release — showed 70.48 ± 0.08% release after 24 h).

010_S7.note_on_table_parsing: 010_S7 row indicates EC=30, other excipients as indicated.

pre_compression_parameters_for_SR_batche.source_table_note: Table III in article. Data are mean ± SD, n = 3.

fourier_transform_infrared_spectral_meas.method: KBr pellet method; 3 mg sample + 300 mg KBr compressed at 10 kg/cm to form pellet; scanned 4000–500 cm^-1 using FT-IR Affinity-1 (Shimadzu).

findings.compatibility_conclusion: All peaks of the optimum formulae (010_F9, 010_F10 and 010_S6) exist in their original position without shifting — indicating no incompatibility between EtM and excipients in the tested formulas.
findings.note: FT-IR spectra for 010_S6 (SR) and 010_F9/010_F10 (ODTs) are included in the article's figures; the article reports absence of drug–excipient interactions based on the preserved characteristic peaks.

SR_tablet_core_compression.device: Single punch tablet machine (Shakti Pharmatech, Model SSTP-12, India)
SR_tablet_core_compression.compression_force: constant compression force for all formulae (numerical pressure not provided)
SR_tablet_core_compression.EtM_content_in_SR_tablets_mg: 40 mg (where specified in the SR table rows)

process_conditions_temperature_pressure.temperature_control: room temperature for compression (not numerically specified)

coating.coating_for_core_in_cup: The core is compressed and the cup is formed by lyophilized ODT surrounding the core—no additional coating process described.

tablet_properties_per_batch_S1_to_S9.source_table_note: Table IV in article. Means ± SD are provided there (n not explicitly repeated for each test).

SR_tablet_testing_conditions.two_stage_dissolution: 0–2 h in 900 mL 0.1 N HCl (pH 1.2), then transfer to phosphate buffer pH 6.8 (900 mL) for the remainder (up to 24 h).
SR_tablet_testing_conditions.apparatus: USP dissolution apparatus (not further numerically specified in Methods beyond apparatus type); paddle
SR_tablet_testing_conditions.sampling: 5 mL aliquots withdrawn at predetermined intervals and replaced with fresh medium; samples filtered (0.22 µm) and assayed spectrophotometrically at λmax=284 nm.

S1_to_S9_release_summary.general_findings: Formulations compressed with either HPMC or MC or EC showed extended release for up to 24 h; type and concentration of matrix-forming polymer affected release (higher polymer concentration decreased release).

S6_specific.article_statement: 010_S6 shows more hindering of the drug release (70.48 ± 0.08%) after 24 h and therefore was selected as the sustained core formula.

full_timepoint_release_profiles.explanation: The article provides release curves (figures) but does not tabulate the full numeric time-by-time dissolution data in the text or tables; only the summary 24-h value for 010_S6 is numerically given explicitly.

release_mechanism_discussion.authors_conclusions: Reduction of EtM release by ethyl cellulose (hydrophobic polymer) is consistent with decreased solvent penetration and diffusion-limited release; authors reference the 'penetration theory' and attribute SR behavior mainly to matrix viscosity and hydrophobicity — i.e., diffusion-controlled matrix release.

tablet_properties_for_B1_and_B2.manufacture_notes: Core tablet prepared by direct compression (SR formula 010_S6 for core). The compressed core tablet was placed in lyophilized ODT pocket and additional solution added to sandwich the core followed by freezing and lyophilization — producing core-in-cup tablets 010_B1 and B2.

weight_thickness_hardness_friability_for.explanation: Article gives characterization for ODTs (F series) and SR (S series) separately in tables, but does not tabulate final 010_B1/010_B2 hardness/thickness/friability explicitly. Pharmacokinetic experiments used tablets sized/adjusted for rats (30 mg tablets administered to rats).

group_treatments.Group_III: MST® marketed product (morphine sulfate) as comparator

in_vivo_study_design_summary.bioanalysis: Plasma EtM measured by HPLC (method described in article). Non-compartmental analysis using Kinetica software.

statistical_analysis_for_in_vivo.method: One-way ANOVA followed by post-hoc multiple comparisons using LSD; significance at p < 0.05.
statistical_analysis_for_in_vivo.reported_findings: Cmax and AUC for 010_B1 and 010_B2 were significantly higher than MST® (p < 0.05). No significant difference between 010_B1 and 010_B2 (p > 0.1) for Cmax.

stability_data.explanation: Article concludes formulation was successful and suggests further human in vivo bioavailability studies; no accelerated or long-term stability numeric data presented.

tablet_performance_and_stability.dissolution_profile_stability_notes: Article presents dissolution curves in figures for ODT and SR formulas; numeric time series data for each time point not tabulated.

experimental_data_in_vitro_and_statistic.micromeritic_statistics: Micromeritic values reported as mean ± SD (n=3).
experimental_data_in_vitro_and_statistic.tablet_characterization_statistics: Weight, thickness, breaking force, tensile strength, friability, drug content reported as mean ± SD in Table IV. Disintegration time reported for ODTs (not applicable to SR).

performance_metrics_summary.S6_as_selected_SR: 010_S6 showed most sustained release (70.48 ± 0.08% at 24 h).
performance_metrics_summary.ODT_selection_for_cup: 010_F9 and 010_F10 selected as cup ODTs based on fastest disintegration time and highest immediate release (note: F-series not requested for extraction per your choice A).

release_kinetics_for_all_formulations.authors_interpretation: Release shows polymer-dependent sustained-release behavior consistent with matrix diffusion/penetration theory; EC produced more sustained profiles than MC/HPMC.

explicit_missing_numeric_data: Complete time-by-time dissolution numeric tables for 010_S1–010_S9 (except 010_S6 24-h point) are not provided in text/tables.
Kinetic model constants (zero/first order rate constants, Korsmeyer-Peppas n and k) are not tabulated in the article.
Compression pressure in numeric units (kN or MPa) for direct compression is not provided.
Final 010_B1/010_B2 physical attributes (hardness, friability, thickness, weight variation) are not tabulated explicitly in the article.
additional_notes_and_missing_items.where_figures_show_data: Several findings (e.g., full dissolution curves, FTIR spectra, SEM images) are presented as figures in the article but the raw numeric files or tables are not present in the text; the JSON indicates where numeric values are missing.

references_and_citation.primary_article: This JSON was generated from the uploaded article. See file citation above: :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}
references_and_citation.note: All numeric values and text summaries were transcribed from the article's tables and text (Tables II–V and Figs described).

article_metadata.title: Design and evaluation of effervescent floating tablets based on hydroxyethyl cellulose and sodium alginate using pentoxifylline as a model drug
article_metadata.overall_conclusion: Effervescent floating matrix tablets containing pentoxifylline with HEC and sodium alginate were successfully prepared; formulations with 20% w/w sodium bicarbonate at 50–54 N hardness showed acceptable floating, swelling, and sustained release over 24 h in 0.1 N HCl (pH 1.2). [file:1]

sections_location.introduction: After abstract, describes gastroretentive systems and rationale for pentoxifylline and floating tablets. [file:1]

materials_and_methods.materials: Subsection 'Materials' lists origin of pentoxifylline, sodium alginate, sodium bicarbonate, Prosolv 90, hydroxyethyl cellulose (Natrosol 250-HHX), magnesium stearate. [file:1]
materials_and_methods.granulation_and_tablet_preparation: Subsections 'Granulation', 'Powder mixtures and granules evaluation', 'Tablets preparation', and 'Evaluation of tablets'. [file:1]
materials_and_methods.FTIR_DSC: Subsections 'Differential scanning calorimetry study' and 'Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy'. [file:1]
materials_and_methods.in_vitro_tests: Subsections on 'Tablet floating capacity', 'Swelling and erosion studies', 'In vitro drug release studies', 'Release data modeling and analysis', and 'Statistical analysis'. [file:1]

sections_location.results_and_discussion: From 'Results and discussion' onward: moisture content, Carr’s index, DSC, FTIR, physical properties, floating, swelling, erosion, dissolution, and modeling. [file:1]
sections_location.conclusion: Final 'Conclusion' section summarizing optimal formulation and behavior. [file:1]

properties_mentioned.half_life: Approximately 1–2 h (short half-life) used as model highly water-soluble, high-density drug
properties_mentioned.dose_per_tablet: 60 mg per tablet for both 011_F1 and 011_F2

sodium_alginate.particle_size_used: 350 µm cut to improve compression compared with 180 µm cut

sodium_bicarbonate.role: Effervescent gas-forming agent to generate 011_CO2 for floating

experimental_design_for_optimization.objective: To design and evaluate effervescent floating gastro-retentive matrix tablets with sustained release using mixtures of hydroxyethyl cellulose and sodium alginate, and to study the effects of granulation, sodium bicarbonate level, and tablet hardness. [file:1]

independent_variables.name: tablet_hardness
levels_N: 59–64 (Level C; attainable only from powder, not from granules for 011_F1/011_F2)

independent_variables.name: production_route
independent_variables.purpose: To compare flow, density, floating capacity, and release behavior before and after granulation.

responses_measured: Moisture content and Carr’s index (flow properties)
Tablet hardness, friability, weight uniformity, content uniformity
Floating lag time and total floating duration
Swelling and erosion (% medium uptake, % mass loss)
Dissolution profile (% drug released vs time in 0.1 N HCl, pH 1.2, 24 h)
Release kinetics (Korsmeyer–Peppas model parameters n and Kp)
Statistical significance via paired t-test and ANOVA

tests: post hoc multiple comparisons when needed

powder_preparation.sieving: All powders passed through 180 µm sieve; sodium alginate used at 350 µm cut to mitigate compression problems. [file:1]
powder_preparation.mixing: Turbula mixer at 60 rpm for 10 min for powder blends. [file:1]

wet_granulation.binder: 0.5% w/w water added to powder mixture
wet_granulation.equipment: Kenwood Chef/Kneader mixer, 10 min mixing, mass passed through 1000 µm sieve
wet_granulation.drying: Drying oven at 60 °C overnight; then granules collected at 853 µm size fraction

granule_evaluation.moisture_content: Measured using Mettler Toledo 011_HG53 halogen moisture analyzer; values reported as mean ± SD for 011_F1 and 011_F2 pre- and post-granulation. [file:1]
granule_evaluation.flow_properties: Carr’s index calculated from bulk and tapped densities using tapping apparatus (Copley JV1000). [file:1]

UV_calibration.method: UV–Vis spectrophotometry at 274 nm in 0.1 N HCl for pentoxifylline; calibration curve prepared with triplicate measurements. [file:1]

011_F1.description: Floating matrix tablet with 10% w/w sodium bicarbonate.

composition_per_tablet_mg.magnesium_stearate: small amount to reach total tablet weight; difference due to lubricant content

approximate_fraction_of_tablet_mass.sodium_bicarbonate_percent: 10% w/w relative to matrix composition as stated by authors

011_F2.description: Floating matrix tablet with 20% w/w sodium bicarbonate.

composition_per_tablet_mg.magnesium_stearate: small amount to reach total tablet weight; difference due to lubricant content

approximate_fraction_of_tablet_mass.sodium_bicarbonate_percent: 20% w/w relative to matrix composition as stated by authors

nonfloating_control.description: Similar HEC–sodium alginate matrix tablets without sodium bicarbonate (0% w/w) used as control in swelling, erosion, and release modeling.
nonfloating_control.detailed_composition: Not fully tabulated in text; assumed to contain same polymer–filler–drug ratios as 011_F1/011_F2 without gas-former.

carr_index_F1.granules: Improved to fair flow (~17%; significant improvement, P < 0.05)

design_matrix.floating: Floating formulations when NaHCO3 used; 011_F1 tablets floated for 8–12 h depending on route and hardness.
011_F2 tablets floated for 24 h at both hardness levels for granule-based and powder-based tablets.
Nonfloating, used for comparison of swelling and erosion.

ftir_measurements.instrument: Perkin Elmer FT-IR system, Spectrum BX series
ftir_measurements.sample_presentation: Small quantity placed on sample stage and compressed by twisting arm; spectra acquired via Spectrum BX software. [file:1]

compatibility_results.F1_F2_powder_and_granules: Pentoxifylline characteristic peaks appeared at similar wavenumbers in mixtures and granules, indicating no chemical incompatibility with excipients.
compatibility_results.conclusion: No new peaks or major shifts indicative of strong interactions; formulation components are compatible.

equipment.tablet_press: Single-punch tableting machine Type 3, Manesty Machines Ltd, UK, with flat-faced 9.60 mm punches.
equipment.dissolution_apparatus: USP Apparatus II, Erweka GmbH, Germany.

consolidation_index_porosity.apparent_density: Increased with hardness and decreased after granulation, depending on formulation and NaHCO3 level. [file:1]
consolidation_index_porosity.porosity_percent: Decreased with higher hardness and with granulation; higher NaHCO3 reduced porosity further.

compression_process.machine: Single-punch Manesty Type 3, flat-faced 9.60 mm tools.
compression_process.process: Automatic compression from granules and manual compression from powder blends to achieve desired hardness levels.
compression_process.hardness_control: Adjusted via compression force to reach levels A–C; level C not achievable post-granulation for 011_F1/011_F2 due to elastic recovery of sodium alginate.

coating.status: No tablet coating reported; tablets are uncoated matrices.

environment_conditions.FTIR_conditions: Room temperature; spectra collected from 4000–620 cm−1.
environment_conditions.dissolution_temperature: 37 ± 0.5 °C in 0.1 N HCl (pH 1.2), paddle speed 50 rpm.

medium_and_conditions.sink_conditions: Maintained; solubility of pentoxifylline is high relative to dose.

F1_granule_tablets.total_floating_duration_h: ≈8 h (011_F1 granule tablets sank before 24 h).

level_A.mechanism: Anomalous (non-Fickian) transport with contributions from diffusion and polymer relaxation.
Anomalous (non-Fickian) with more pronounced polymer relaxation.

release_kinetics_summary.overall: Most granule-based formulations (floating and nonfloating) exhibit non-Fickian (anomalous) release as per the Korsmeyer–Peppas model, indicating combined diffusion and polymer relaxation/erosion. Increasing hardness tends to reduce Kp for powder-based tablets; granulation generally lowers Kp and increases n. [file:1]
release_kinetics_summary.zero_first_order: The article focuses on Korsmeyer–Peppas modeling rather than fitting detailed zero-order/first-order parameters; numeric zero/first-order constants are not tabulated.

swelling_water_uptake.method: Determined by medium uptake (DMU) at time intervals up to 24 h; tablets withdrawn, blotted, weighed wet, then dried at 60 °C to constant weight.

results_summary.trend_F2: Higher NaHCO3 (20% w/w) produced lower net swelling but higher mass loss due to more effervescence and longer floating, compared with nonfloating tablets and F1.
results_summary.trend_F1: 011_F1 granule tablets showed higher medium uptake near 24 h than 011_F2 when they had sunk and all surfaces were exposed.
results_summary.nonfloating: Nonfloating tablets showed highest swelling but lowest mass loss, as they remained fully submerged without effervescence.

tablet_mechanical_properties.hardness: All 011_F1/011_F2 granule tablets at levels A and B showed hardness around 5–6 kg with low variability (SD < 0.5 kg). [file:1]
tablet_mechanical_properties.friability: All <1% weight loss, meeting BP requirements; 011_F2 slightly higher friability at level A due to higher NaHCO3 content.
tablet_mechanical_properties.weight_variation: Twenty tablets tested for each formulation; deviations within BP limits.
tablet_mechanical_properties.thickness_and_diameter: Diameter fixed at 9.60 mm; thickness decreased slightly with increased hardness.

dissolution_profile.qualitative_F1: Granule-based 011_F1 tablets at level A released drug more slowly than powder-based 011_F1 at same hardness; increasing hardness reduced release rate. 011_F1 provided sustained release up to 24 h but with incomplete floating duration.
dissolution_profile.qualitative_F2: Granule-based 011_F2 at level A showed slower release than 011_F1 and nonfloating tablets due to longer floating and lower swelling. Increasing NaHCO3 from 10 to 20% decreased release rate for granule-based tablets but increased it for powder-based tablets (due to pore formation from gas).

stability.formal_stability_study: Not reported; study focuses on immediate post-manufacture performance, not long-term stability.

examples: Granulation significantly reduced moisture content and Carr’s index (P < 0.05) for both 011_F1 and F2.
Granulation significantly affected apparent density, porosity, floating lag time, and release profile in many cases (P < 0.05).
Raising NaHCO3 from 10 to 20% significantly changed floating lag time and drug release profiles for powder-based tablets; effect was attenuated after granulation.
Korsmeyer–Peppas fits showed R² values mostly >0.98, supporting the chosen model.

article_metadata.title: Denatured Whey Protein Powder as a New Matrix Excipient: Design and Evaluation of Mucoadhesive Tablets for Sustained Drug Release Applications

sections_likely_locations.materials_origin: Materials; Preparation of DWP Powders; Characterization of DWP Powders
sections_likely_locations.experimental_design_for_optimization: Preparation of Matrix Tablets; Characterization of DWP Matrix Tablets; Evaluation of Drug Release; Analysis of Drug Release Mechanisms; Artificial Digestion of Drug Loaded-Tablets
sections_likely_locations.ingredients_identification_absorbance: Materials; Preparation of DWP Powders; Characterization of DWP Powders (solubility by UV at 280 nm; theophylline at 273 nm)
sections_likely_locations.formulation_details: Preparation of Matrix Tablets; Table I
sections_likely_locations.precompression_parameters: Physical Characteristics and Compressibility of Powders; Powders Flow Properties; Table I
sections_likely_locations.independent_variables_and_levels: Preparation of Matrix Tablets; Table I
sections_likely_locations.tablet_fabrication: Preparation of Matrix Tablets; Pharmacotechnical Characterization
sections_likely_locations.process_parameters: Preparation of DWP Powders; Preparation of Matrix Tablets; In Vitro Drug Dissolution Tests; Sequential Drug Release Kinetics
sections_likely_locations.release_properties: Evaluation of Drug Release (Dissolution Profiles in Enzyme Free Buffers; Influence of pH Shift; Influence of Gastric and Intestinal Enzymes); Analysis of Drug Release Mechanisms
sections_likely_locations.tablet_properties: Pharmacotechnical Characterization; Swelling and Erosion Studies; Mucoadhesive Behavior; Evaluation of Drug Release
sections_likely_locations.experimental_data: Results and Discussion throughout (Physical Characteristics, Swelling/Erosion, Mucoadhesion, Dissolution, Modeling, Artificial Digestive System)

whey_protein_isolate.description: Whey protein isolate (WPI 89.5%) used to prepare native and denatured whey protein solutions

denatured_whey_protein_powder_preparatio.starting_material: WPI powder rehydrated in deionized water to 10% w/w
denatured_whey_protein_powder_preparatio.rehydration_conditions: Gentle magnetic stirring 1 h at room temperature, then 2 h rest

experimental_design_for_optimization.goal: Evaluate feasibility of direct compression tablets using DWP powder as matrix/mucoadhesive excipient and study their controlled release behavior with theophylline.
experimental_design_for_optimization.design_type: Formulation screening with increasing DWP content in directly compressed matrix tablets; multiple in vitro dissolution conditions and mathematical modeling.

formulations.code_scheme: 012_F0–012_F5 for drug-loaded tablets; F0a–F4a for placebo (no drug) tablets.
formulations.independent_variable: DWP percentage in the tablet blend (0, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40% nominal for 012_F0–012_F5).
responses: Powder flow and compressibility (bulk/tapped density, CI, HR)
Tablet hardness, friability, weight, disintegration time
Swelling and erosion (water uptake, volume change, matrix weight loss)
Theophylline dissolution in various pH media with/without enzymes
Release kinetics parameters (Higuchi, Korsmeyer-Peppas, first order, Weibull)
Mucoadhesion on excised pig intestinal mucosa
Drug release/absorption in the artificial digestive system TIM-1 for 012_F4

proteins.method: Excess NWP or DWP dispersed in water or buffers (pH 1.2, 4.5, 6.8), 24 h equilibration, centrifugation, spectrophotometric assay.
proteins.analysis: Protein concentration in supernatant from calibration curves to calculate solubility and DWP release.

standard_dissolution.apparatus: UV spectrophotometry on dissolution samples from USP 2 paddle tests

sequential_media_with_protein.mobile_phase: 100% eluent A: 9% acetonitrile in 0.01 M acetate buffer pH 4

formulation_details_each_batch.note: Quantitative tablet composition is given in Table I; the article uses 012_F0–012_F5, not 012_F0–F6, and 012_F5 (40% DWP) was not successfully tableted for all tests.

012_F1.mcc_qs: Balance after adding the above components

012_F4.comment: Selected as optimized; used in mucoadhesion tests and TIM-1.

placebo_batches.F0a_to_F4a: Same composition as 012_F0–012_F4 but theophylline replaced by equivalent mass of MCC; used for whey protein release and erosion tests.

precompression_parameters_each_batch.note: Reported in Table I as blend properties, not strictly ‘pre-compression’ tablet data.

tablet_mechanical_properties.note: Hardness and friability reported for 012_F0–012_F4 tablets; values are means with SD where provided.

controlled_variables: Compression tooling: 12 mm flat cylindrical punches
Target hardness: ≥ 60 N; typically around 70–85 N

blend_preparation: Mix theophylline with DWP and MCC for 20 min.
Add magnesium stearate (1% w/w) and Aerosil (0.5% w/w) and mix additional 10 min.

compression.equipment: Alternative tableting press machine (Korsch, Berlin, Germany)
compression.mode: Direct compression of final powder blend

equipment_pharmacotechnical_tests.swelling_erosion: USP 2 dissolution vessels with paddle, 1000 mL, 37 °C, 60 rpm

batch_specific_tablet_properties.note: Density and consolidation index not explicitly given; CI refers to powder compressibility index, not tablet consolidation index.

compression_process.device: Korsch single-station/alternative tableting press (type not further specified)
compression_process.pressure: Not explicitly reported; hardness controlled to ≥ 60 N.

media: Simulated gastric fluid (SGF) with and without pepsin
Simulated intestinal fluid (SIF) with and without pancreatin
dissolution_test_conditions.sampling: 1 mL, replaced by fresh medium; up to 12 h.

sequential_dissolution_protocol.analysis: Theophylline quantified by RP-HPLC at 273 nm.

artificial_digestive_system_TIM1.pH_control: Computer-controlled HCl (0.3 M) and NaHCO3 (0.5 M) additions
artificial_digestive_system_TIM1.dose_introduced: One 012_F4 tablet with 200 g of water for fasted conditions.

general.media: USP buffers pH 1.2, 4.5, 6.8; SGF/SIF with and without enzymes; TIM-1.
general.overall_mechanism_summary: Primarily diffusion-controlled in enzyme-free media; shifts toward erosion-accelerated release in presence of proteolytic enzymes.

release_profile.enzyme_free: Immediate-release: >80% drug in <45 min in all pH; complete release by 2–3 h.
release_profile.sequential: Similar to single pH tests; no pH-shift effects.

release_kinetics.dominant_model: First-order/Higuchi typical of IR matrix with soluble drug
release_kinetics.mechanism: Dissolution of theophylline from non-gelling MCC matrix.

release_profile.enzyme_free: Slower than 012_F0 but still relatively fast; incomplete release at pH 4.5 (~70% at 6 h, no disintegration up to 6 h); ~complete in 24 h at pH 1.2 and 6.8.
release_profile.sequential_without_enzymes: Similar shape to single pH but 012_T50 and 012_T80 extended; compacted structure at pH 4.5 impacts subsequent release.
release_profile.with_enzymes: Marked acceleration; release fits Korsmeyer-Peppas with higher kKP, n ≈ 0.66; erosion more prominent.

release_profile.enzyme_free: Clear sustained release; 012_T50 ~ 6 h, 012_T80 ~ 16 h; complete release by 24 h; similar profiles at pH 1.2 and 6.8, slightly slower at pH 4.5.
release_profile.sequential_without_enzymes: Further retardation vs individual pH; 012_T50 extended to ~8 h.
release_profile.with_enzymes: Faster and more complete release; erosion-driven plus diffusion.

release_profile.enzyme_free: Similar to 012_F2 and 012_F4; controlled release with 012_T50 ~ 6 h, 012_T80 ~ 16 h, complete by 24 h across pH.
release_profile.with_enzymes: Faster release; erosion pronounced; complete release by ~8 h.

release_profile.enzyme_free: Robust sustained release; 012_T50 ~ 6 h, 012_T80 ~ 16 h, complete release in 24 h; similar profiles at pH 1.2 and 6.8, slightly slower at pH 4.5 due to proximity to protein pI.
release_profile.sequential_without_enzymes: 012_T50 extended to ~8 h due to shrinkage/compaction at pH 4.5 influencing subsequent pH 6.8 stage.
release_profile.with_enzymes: The most resistant among DWP formulations; still accelerated release vs enzyme-free but with slower erosion; complete release by ~8 h.

table_IIIb.comment: Erosion and diffusion both contribute; kKP increased vs enzyme-free.

free_theophylline.profile: Fast absorption typical of BCS I.

drug_loaded_dwp_powder.profile: Spray-dried powder does not significantly slow release.

tablet_F4.profile: Clear sustained absorption; confirms matrix functionality under dynamic GIT conditions.

012_F5.comment: Not taken forward into tablet dissolution studies due to poor flow and inability to compress robust tablets.

F1_10_percent.behavior: High water uptake leading to disintegration and erosion; tablets disintegrated after 4 h at pH 1.2 and 6.8 and after 6 h at pH 4.5.
F1_10_percent.DWP_release: Pronounced; >65% released by 6 h at pH 1.2 and 6.8; ~50% at pH 4.5.

F2_to_F4_15_to_30_percent.behavior: Rapid water uptake (~150% within 30 min) with strong swelling; matrix weight loss limited (DWP release <10% by 12 h); gel layer forms; inner core remains intact up to 4 h then fully gelled.
F2_to_F4_15_to_30_percent.threshold_effect: Above 20% DWP, water uptake becomes limited and swelling somewhat reduced; 012_F4 shows lower water uptake than 012_F2–F3.

mucoadhesion.test: Wash-off test on porcine intestinal mucosa at 37 °C in pH 6.8 buffer with up-and-down motion (USP disintegration apparatus).

012_F2.adhesion_result: ≈33% of tablets still adhered at 15 min; lower mucoadhesion.

012_F4.adhesion_result: 100% of tablets adhered up to 60 min; strong mucoadhesive properties.

mucoadhesion.mechanism: Mucoadhesion attributed to DWP denaturation exposing free thiol and hydrophilic groups, enabling hydrogen bonding and disulfide bonds with mucin; higher DWP content increases available functional groups.

tablet_properties_and_swelling.stability: No long-term stability study of tablets reported; only in vitro performance and TIM-1 digestion.

dwp_release_from_placebo_tablets.result: DWP release almost zero up to 4 h for ≥15% DWP; ~7% after 12 h; no pH influence.

in_vitro.kinetic_modeling: Weibull, Higuchi, Korsmeyer-Peppas, first-order fits; see 012_F1–012_F4 entries and Table III for representative r² and parameters.

in_vivo_or_ex_vivo.mucoadhesion_ex_vivo: Adhesion percentages for 012_F2 vs 012_F4 on pig intestine; 012_F4 clearly superior.
in_vivo_or_ex_vivo.tim1_ads: Level-A style correlation context cited; 012_F4 tablets show extended absorption vs free drug.

abstract: High-level formulation approach (wet granulation with HPMC K100M, Kollidon SR, Keltone LVCR, lactose).
Key release result (best formulation and release duration, release model mentioned).
introduction: General background on sustained-release tablets and advantages.
Process overview for sustained-release tablets (direct compression, wet granulation).
materials_and_methods: Materials origin and isolation of mangiferin.
Experimental design for preparation of tablets 013_F1–F12.
Tablet fabrication process (wet granulation procedure, equipment, punch size, target hardness).

Pre-compression and post-compression evaluation methods (angle of repose, bulk/tapped density, Carr’s index, Hausner ratio, hardness, friability, weight variation, thickness, content uniformity, dissolution conditions).
results_and_discussion: Pre-compression parameters statement and resulting Table 5 (thickness, hardness, friability, assay).
Statement of optimized formulation (013_F5) and its performance including release kinetics model and mechanism.
conclusion: Optimized polymer level in core to control mangiferin release.
Qualitative description of pH-independent release and compliance with USP acceptance.
General statement on effect of polymer concentration on release.

active_pharmaceutical_ingredient.name: Mangiferin
