tablet_properties.lag_time: Coated tablets with 80:20 Surelease:Opadry ratio and 3.5% WG exhibited an initial lag time of about 0.5-1 hour in vitro. In vivo, lag time increased as coating permeability decreased.

in_vitro_release_data.F1_K100LV_core: Fastest release rate among viscosity grades.
in_vitro_release_data.F2_K4M_core: Moderate release rate. Release faster in early stage (0-8h) than late stage (8-20h).
in_vitro_release_data.F4_25%K4M: Fastest release among different HPMC amounts.
in_vitro_release_data.F8_80:20_coated: Initial lag time (0.5-1h) followed by linear release.
in_vitro_release_data.F10_60:40_coated: Lag time eliminated, but release rate decreased in final period compared to F8.
in_vitro_release_data.F12_5.5%WG: Longer lag time and slower release than F11.
in_vitro_release_data.F13_7.5%WG: Longest lag time and slowest release among weight gains.

experimental_data.zero_and_first_order_release: Zero-order kinetics were observed for coated tablets (R² > 0.99). Uncoated tablets followed Power Law kinetics (non-Fickian), with n values between 0.46 and 0.89.

ingredients_identification.ez_nlc_preparation_method: The oily phase (stearic acid and oleic acid, 30%) was melted at 348°K. The aqueous phase (1% Tween 80 and 2% poloxamer 188 in distilled water) was heated to the same temperature. 10mg EZ was added to the molten lipid. The aqueous phase was added dropwise to the oily phase under magnetic stirring at 1400rpm and 333°K for 15min. The pre-emulsion was ultrasonicated using a probe sonicator at 100% amplitude for 5min. The dispersion was mixed with drying excipients (Avicel alone or with mannitol) (5-7.5 w/%) until a homogeneous paste was formed. The paste was frozen at 253°K overnight and lyophilized (pre-freezing at 193°K for 15min, ramp 20min, main drying at 3 mbar for 6h). The resulting powder was mixed with 10mg magnesium stearate, 10mg talc, and CCS.

formulation_details.note: The specific mass of drying excipients and CCS for each of the 13 individual batches were not listed separately in the text but were varied according to the experimental design levels.

pre_compression_parameters.summary_statistics: All formulations showed good flowability according to USP 39/NF 34. ANOVA showed no significant difference between formulations.

fourier_transform_infrared_spectral_meas.findings: Characteristic peaks of EZ were seen in the EZ-NLCs tablet spectrum and disappeared in the blank NLCs tablet spectrum, confirming encapsulation and lack of interaction.

release_properties.summary: The release profiles showed a biphasic pattern. Tablets with 30mg CCS followed First-Order kinetics. Tablets with 15mg CCS followed Higuchi kinetics.

experimental_data.in_vitro_release_findings: EZ-NLCs tablets showed higher release than ezetrol®. In first 2h (pH 1.2), release ranged from 53.84% to 90.6% (vs 48.3% for ezetrol). At 24h, release ranged from 87.4% to 99% (vs 67.17% for ezetrol). Optimized tablet released 98.3 ± 3.95% in 24h.

in_vivo_pharmacokinetics.study_design: Parallel, single oral dose of 25mg/kg in Sprague Dawley male rats (n=3 per group).
in_vivo_pharmacokinetics.conclusion: EZ-NLCs tablet showed 1.5-fold improvement in oral bioavailability compared to ezetrol®.

materials_origin.other_chemicals: All other chemicals and solvents used were of analytical grade.

experimental_design_for_optimization.ingredients_identification: Solid dispersions were prepared with lipophilic carriers (compritol 045_ATO888, Gelucire 43/01, Gelucire 39/01, precirol ATO 05). Neusillin 045_US2 was used as an adsorbent. The solid dispersions were compressed into minitablets (20 mg) and filled into size '0' capsules.
experimental_design_for_optimization.results: Formulations 045_F9, 045_F10, 045_F14, and 045_F15 showed instantaneous floating lag time (0 min), floating time >12 h, and sustained release up to 12 h. Pharmacokinetic study of 045_F9 showed 2.46 times increase in AUC with increased residence time.
experimental_design_for_optimization.absorbance_measurement: UV spectrophotometric analysis at 273 nm for drug content and in vitro dissolution studies.

composition_of_independent_variables.levels: Drug to lipophilic carrier ratios of 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, 1:4, and 1:5.
composition_of_independent_variables.adsorbent_ratio: Lipophilic carrier to Neusillin 045_US2 is maintained at 1:0.5.

ftir_spectral_measurements.method: Potassium bromide discs, scanning speed of 2 mm/s with a resolution of 4 cm-1 over the region 4000-400 cm-1.
ftir_spectral_measurements.findings: Pure drug showed absorption bands at 3181 cm-1 (NH group), 3040 cm-1 (OH group), and 2740 cm-1 (045_OCH3 group). Dispersion granules with excipients showed absorption bands in a similar range, indicating compatibility.

content.details: Tara gum (TG) isolate and other excipients (talc, Aerosil, magnesium stearate, lactose 046_DCL21, sodium starch glycolate) were obtained from Vineet Analytical Research Laboratories Pvt Ltd. (Pioma Chemicals, Mumbai, Maharashtra, India).

Tablets containing imatinib were developed using TG extract. Conventional tablets were made with a variable ratio of excipients to TG utilizing the direct compression process. All excipients were well-mixed except magnesium stearate. Following adequate mixing of the extract and other ingredients, Aerosil and magnesium stearate were added and combined for a further 2-3 min.

content.source: Materials and methods, Phytochemical screening, Content uniformity sections

details.preparation: The optimized tablet formulation was used for the initial phytochemical per standard procedures [18]. For content uniformity, 10 tablets from each formulation were ground into a fine powder. Part of the powder (20 mg of imatinib) was dissolved in methanol, filtered, and appropriately diluted.
details.results: Phytochemical screening revealed the presence of saponins, tannins, phenolics, flavonoids, carbohydrates, proteins, and amino acids. Steroids, alkaloids, fats, and oils were absent.
details.absorbance: Absorbance for drug content and dissolution studies was measured at 238 nm using a UV-visible spectrophotometer.

experimental_design_for_optimization.purpose: To design a pulsatile formulation of lornoxicam to treat rheumatoid arthritis as per the chronotherapeutic pattern of the disease, targeting drug release in the early morning hours.
experimental_design_for_optimization.approach: Two-step process: 1) Preparation of fast-disintegrating core tablets with varying disintegrant (crospovidone) concentration. 2) Compression coating of the optimized core tablet with different ratios and combinations of hydrophobic (ethyl cellulose) and hydrophilic (HPMC K4M, L-hpc, NaCMC) polymers to achieve a lag time of ~5.5 hours.
experimental_design_for_optimization.optimized_formulation: F-5, which released lornoxicam after a lag time of 5.5 ± 0.7 hours and 99.81 ± 0.81% up to 8 hours.

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Physical mixture of drug and polymers.

key_results: Core tablets with 10% crospovidone (LCT-4) showed fastest disintegration (1.52 min) and 99.93% drug release in 30 min.
Optimized formulation F-5 (L-hpc:EC 150:150 mg) provided a lag time of 5.5 ± 0.7 h and 99.81 ± 0.81% release by 8 h.
Order of extending lag time: HPMC-K4M > L-hpc > NaCMC.
FTIR and DSC confirmed no drug-polymer interaction.
F-5 was stable for 6 months at 40°C/75% RH as per ICH guidelines.

article_sections.abstract: The present study aimed to formulate and evaluate the formulated sustained-release floating matrix tablets of valganciclovir hydrochloride to produce a stable and bioavailable dosage form. The tablets were prepared using hydrophilic and hydrophobic polymers such as ethyl cellulose, Hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC), and Povidone. The formulations were subjected to evaluation characteristics such as drug content, hardness, friability, floating lag time, total floating time, and In vitro drug dissolution studies. The formulation composition and method of manufacturing are novel for this particular active moiety and robustness was assessed using a central composite design. All the formulation trials exhibited more than 90% of drug release in 12 h duration, with a floating lag time of more than 11 h, and drug content was found more than 90% across the batches. The formulated valganciclovir hydrochloride sustained release floating matrix tablets showed an increased GRT with a sustained release for 12 h, thereby allowing a better window for absorption and consequently improving the drug's therapeutic effect.
article_sections.introduction: Provides background on cytomegalovirus, valganciclovir hydrochloride, and gastroretentive drug delivery systems. Highlights the aim to formulate sustained-release floating matrix tablets.
article_sections.materials_and_methods: Lists materials, methodology including melting point, solubility, calibration curve, drug-excipient compatibility, drug content, wet granulation formulation, compression, in vitro dissolution, floating lag time determination.
article_sections.results_and_discussion: Presents melting point, solubility, λmax, FTIR compatibility, formulation development using central composite design, hardness/friability/assay results, dissolution profiles, floating lag time and total floating time.
article_sections.conclusion: Confirms purity, compatibility, ideal in-process parameters, robustness, and reproducibility of the optimized formulation.

general.experimental_design: pH-controlled two-pulse delivery system. Core tablets (216 mg, 200 mg amoxicillin) were compression-coated with barrier layers containing HPMC (100, 400, 4000 cps) and spray-dried lactose (SDL) as pore former in varying ratios. Final two-pulse inlay tablets combined an immediate-release outer layer (300 mg amoxicillin) and the barrier-coated core (200 mg amoxicillin). Batches 055_C1–055_C12 represent the compression-coated tablets (core + barrier layer).

ftir_dsc_summary.dsc: Endothermic peaks: pure amoxicillin 298.9 °C, formulation (055_C4) 294.4 °C; negligible shift, no interaction.

stability_study.results: No significant difference in drug release (95% CI). Drug content 48.14 ± 0.35% (likely for barrier-coated core). Hardness within limits (6.4 ± 0.23 kg/cm²).

general.statistical_analysis: One-way ANOVA with Tukey's post hoc test, confidence limit 95%.

060_Formulation-development-and-characte.study_title: FORMULATION DEVELOPMENT AND CHARACTERIZATION OF GASTROTENTIVE DRUG DELIVERY SYSTEMS OF LORATADINE
060_Formulation-development-and-characte.authors: SRINU NAIK SAPAVATU, RAJENDRA KUMAR JADIP

sections.abstract: The main objective of the research work is to develop a single unit non-effervescent drug delivery system of Loratadine (LTD) by direct compression process to prolong the gastric residence time (GRT). LTD non-effervescent floating tablets were prepared with different polymers like hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC) K15M, HPMC K100M (i.e.: 1:1, 1:2, 1:3) as release retardants. Glyceryl behenate (Compritol 888 ATO) and Glyceryl palmitostearate (Precirol ATO 5) were used (1:1, 1:2, 1:3) as low-density lipids to impart buoyancy for longer period. The drug (LTD) and excipient (i.e. HPMC, low-density lipid aids, etc.) interaction studies were carried out by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) and there was no likely interaction involving them. The developed LTD floating matrix tablets were characterized by pre and post-compression parameters and all results were found within the pharmacopoeial limits. The cumulative percentage of drug release ranges from 56.87 ± 0.25% (060_F12) to 99.87 ± 0.09% (060_F2). The drug release profiles of the all formulations (060_F1 to 060_F12) were subjected to various pharmacokinetic parameters and the optimized formulation (060_F3) followed the Korsmeyer Peppas (R²=0.996) model with non-Fickian diffusion (n>0.5). The obtained data by radiographic images of 060_F3 formulation showed the GRT of 6±0.5 h (n=3). Hence, from all evaluation studies, it was evident that 060_F3 formulation was optimized (99.82±1.63 % drug release in 12 h).
sections.introduction: LTD is a lipophilic, non-sedating 060_H1 blocker used to treat seasonal allergic rhinitis having low bioavailability (40%) and biological half-life (8h). To avoid frequent administration and improve patient compliance, an extended-release floating matrix formulation was planned. Low-density lipids like Compritol and Precirol were used to allow the formulation to stay on top of gastric contents, avoiding first-pass metabolism and improving bioavailability.

060_Formulation-development-and-characte.conclusion: LTD non-effervescent floating tablets were successfully developed using HPMC K15M, HPMC K100M as release retardants and Compritol 888 ATO, Precirol ATO 5 as floating aids. No drug-excipient interactions were found. All formulations showed good physicochemical characteristics. Formulation 060_F3 was optimized based on all parameters, showing 99.82±1.63% drug release in 12 h and a mean gastric residence time of 6±0.5 h from radiographic studies. The formulation can help improve the absorption of LTD by retaining it in the stomach for a longer period.

article_info.title: Formulation of various polysaccharides-based multicoated tablets containing dexamethasone and probiotics for inflammatory bowel disease
article_info.authors: Raditya Iswandana, Kurnia Sari Setio Putri, Hani Nur Anisa, Wilson Ricardo, Puteri Almadhiva Siti Nurhadi

experimental_design.description: Three core tablet formulations (067_F1, 067_F2, 067_F3) with different probiotic concentrations (16%, 40%, 0% of core weight) were prepared by wet granulation. Each core was coated with a primary coating of either sodium alginate (A), chitosan (C), or xanthan gum (X), followed by a secondary coating of Eudragit 067_L100:067_S100 (1:4). Total of 9 coated formulations (F1A, F2A, F3A, F1C, F2C, F3C, F1X, F2X, F3X).

probiotic_concentration.note: Percentage of total core weight (100 mg) occupied by probiotics (L. acidophilus and B. longum in 1:1 ratio).

dependent_variables: Tablet hardness, friability, weight variation, thickness, diameter

core_tablets.description: Core tablets (100 mg target weight) prepared by wet granulation. Ingredients per tablet as per Table 1. Note: PVP is added as a 9% w/w solution in isopropyl alcohol; the quantity listed is the weight of the binder solution expressed as a percentage of the total dry powder weight. The dry PVP content is 9% of that solution weight.

067_F1.PVP_solution_9%: 5.8% w/w of dry powder (approx. 5.8 mg solution)

pre_compression_parameters.note: Values are from Table 4. All granules showed good flow properties.

compression.device: Single punch tablet press (Erweka EP-1, Germany)

coating.device: Coating pan (Erweka DKM, Germany) at 18 rpm
coating.conditions: Spray coating method; primary coating applied first to achieve specified weight gain, then secondary coating applied for an additional 8% weight gain.

disintegration_test.result: All multicoated tablets showed no disintegration, cracking, or softening after 1 hour in simulated gastric fluid (pH 1.2) and after 3 hours in simulated intestinal fluid (pH 7.2). This was attributed to the pH‑dependent solubility of Eudragit 067_S100 (dissolves above pH 7).
disintegration_test.note: Disintegration test was not performed per individual batch; results are general for all coated formulations.

in_vitro_dissolution_method.media: 500 mL 0.1 N HCl (pH 1.2) for 2 hours, then 500 mL phosphate buffer pH 7.2 for 3 hours, then 500 mL phosphate buffer pH 6.8 (CO₂‑purged, anaerobic) for 7 hours

release_kinetics.note: The authors did not fit release data to zero-order, first-order, Hixson-Crowell, or Korsmeyer-Peppas models.

factors_influencing_release.pH: The secondary coating (Eudragit 067_L100/067_S100) is pH‑sensitive; however, high release in acidic medium was observed, attributed to insufficient coating thickness or uneven coating.
factors_influencing_release.probiotics: No significant effect of probiotic concentration on drug release was observed for any of the primary coatings.
factors_influencing_release.enzymes: Not tested in vitro; the study aimed to use colonic microflora enzymes for polysaccharide degradation, but no enzymatic dissolution was performed.

key_results: All multicoated tablets showed substantial drug release (>10%) in simulated gastric fluid (pH 1.2), indicating failure to achieve colon‑targeted delivery.
Probiotic concentration did not significantly affect the release profile for any coating polymer.
Tablets coated with alginate, chitosan, or xanthan gum all exhibited similar release patterns, with nearly complete release by 12 hours.
The secondary coating (Eudragit 067_L100/067_S100) did not adequately protect against release in acidic conditions.
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD) for n=3 (dissolution) or n=10–20 (tablet properties). No inferential statistics (e.g., ANOVA) were reported.
experimental_data.lag_time: Not defined; early release observed from 15 minutes in acidic medium.

067_Formulation-of-various-polysaccharid.notes: The dissolution data for some time points (especially at 720 min) are incomplete in the PDF; values were extracted as available. The table for xanthan gum coated F1X, F2X, F3X likely includes all three formulations based on text, but some values are truncated. The authors concluded that the formulation was not optimal for colon targeting due to premature release.

materials_origin.xanthan_gum_sodium_alginate: Shanghai Rizheng Chemical Technology Co., Ltd., China

equipment.dissolution_apparatus: USP apparatus type II (paddle), 50 rpm, 900 mL 0.1N HCl (pH 1.2), 37 ± 0.5 °C

formulations.release_profile: Compared with 068_F4 (HPMC K15M), 068_F1 showed faster release (similarity factor 068_f2 = 47.563). HPMC K4M provided higher release rate due to lower molecular weight and less viscous gel layer.
Lower polymer concentration led to faster erosion (mucoadhesion time 5 h) and faster release.
Higher concentration of HPMC K4M gave sustained release compared to 068_F1/F2.
HPMC K15M matrix gave controlled release. Dissimilar to 068_F5 and 068_F6 (068_f2 = 35.715 and 46.225 respectively). Slower release than 068_F1 (068_f2 = 47.563) due to higher molecular weight.
Lower HPMC K15M concentration gave faster release compared to 068_F4 and F6.
Highest HPMC K15M concentration gave slowest release among 068_F4–F6.
Faster release than 068_F4 (HPMC K15M) with similarity factor 068_f2 = 46.225 (dissimilar). XG showed initial burst in acidic medium.
Lower XG concentration led to faster erosion (mucoadhesion time 6 h) and faster release compared to F7.
Higher XG concentration gave higher mucoadhesion and more sustained release than 068_F7 and F8.
Sodium alginate with mannitol gave higher drug release compared to 068_F11; similar to 068_F2 (068_f2 = 58.391 and 52.572).
Lactose as diluent increased drug release due to pore formation; dissolution similar to 068_F2 and 068_F10 (068_f2 = 58.391, 52.572).
Wet granulation gave slightly faster release than 068_F7 but not statistically significant (068_f2 = 53.394).
Slower release than 068_F7 (068_f2 = 39.169). Combination of HPMC K4M and MCC gave controlled release.
Slower release compared to 068_F7 (068_f2 = 48.750). Sodium alginate in acidic medium became protonated, reducing drug release.

release_kinetics_summary.general_mechanism: For HPMC matrices, drug release follows diffusion-controlled mechanism, likely Korsmeyer-Peppas model with n value indicating anomalous transport. XG matrices show initial burst in acidic pH. Sodium alginate matrices show reduced release due to protonation.
factors_influencing_release: Polymer molecular weight (higher HPMC MW reduces release)
Polymer concentration (higher concentration reduces release)
Polymer type (XG gives faster initial release; HPMC gives more controlled)
Diluent type (lactose increases release due to pore formation)
pH (acidic medium affects XG and Na-Alg behavior)
Compression method (wet granulation vs direct compression slightly affects release)

swelling_and_mucoadhesion_analysis.mucoadhesion_correlation: Increased polymer concentration and higher molecular weight increase mucoadhesion strength (p < 0.0001).

experimental_design_for_optimization.description: Six sustained-release matrix tablet formulations (069_F1–069_F6) were prepared using wet granulation, varying type and concentration of hydrophilic polymers (HPMC E-15, Carbopol 934, Sodium CMC) and lactose filler to study their effect on rabeprazole release over 10 h.
experimental_design_for_optimization.design_type: Simple comparative formulation study (no formal factorial/RSM optimization reported).
independent_variables: Polymer type: HPMC E-15, Carbopol 934, Sodium CMC
dependent_variables: Flow properties (angle of repose, densities, Carr's index)
experimental_design_for_optimization.optimization_note: No statistical optimization (e.g., design of experiments, regression models) is described; conclusions are based on comparative release profiles and qualitative assessment.

ftir_identification.purpose: Identification of pure rabeprazole and compatibility with polymers.
ftir_identification.method_summary: FT-IR spectrum of rabeprazole was recorded and compared with reference spectrum; mixture spectra (drug + polymers) were examined for interaction.
ftir_identification.results_summary: Wave numbers of pure drug and drug–excipient mixtures were reported as almost similar, indicating no significant interaction and compatibility.
section_sources: Results and Discussion – FT-IR compatibility paragraph

uv_vis_assay_and_calibration.assay_medium: Phosphate buffer pH 7.2 (for estimation of rabeprazole sodium)
uv_vis_assay_and_calibration.assay_description: Rabeprazole sodium dissolved in phosphate buffer pH 7.2 to obtain 10 µg/mL solution, further diluted and scanned between 200–400 nm on a double-beam UV–VIS spectrophotometer using buffer as blank to determine λmax.

calibration_curve_water.stock_solution: 25 mg rabeprazole in 100 mL volumetric flask, volume made up with distilled water.
calibration_curve_water.regression_equation_units: A: absorbance at 287 nm; C: concentration in µg/mL

drug_content_assay_in_tablets.procedure_summary: Three tablets from each batch were placed in 100 mL volumetric flask, volume made up with 0.1 N HCl, kept for 48 h, 1 mL withdrawn, filtered, suitably diluted and analyzed spectrophotometrically (wavelength not re-stated, implied use of same assay).
drug_content_assay_in_tablets.calculation_details: Not fully specified; assumes use of calibration curve/standard absorbance at 287 nm.

formulation_details_per_batch.note: Values are per tablet in mg as given in Table 2; '-' entries in the article are treated as 0 mg.

pre_compression_parameters.note: The article reports angle of repose, bulk density, tapped density, and Carr’s index for granules/tablets by batch; no standard deviations are provided.

independent_variables_and_levels.description: Independent formulation variables are primarily polymer type and polymer amount per tablet; lactose serves as diluent.

sample_types: Physical mixtures of rabeprazole with HPMC E-15, Carbopol 934, Sodium CMC (exact ratios not specified)
key_observations: Characteristic peaks of rabeprazole were retained in mixtures.
No significant shifts or disappearance of major peaks, indicating absence of strong drug–polymer interaction.

fabrication_process.particle_sizing: All polymers and active ingredients passed through sieve no. 80; wet granules passed through sieve no. 20.
fabrication_process.drying_conditions: Granules dried in hot air oven at 45 °C.
fabrication_process.lubrication: Dried granules mixed with magnesium stearate and talc.

compression.tablet_shape_and_size: Not specified; described only as 'desired hardness, shape, and size'.
compression.coating: No tablet coating described; matrix tablets appear to be uncoated.

friability.method: Roche friabilator, approximately 6 g dedusted tablets, 100 free falls of 6 inches at 25 rpm.

weight_variation.method: 20 tablets weighed individually, average weight calculated; deviation expressed as % weight variation.

thickness.method: Crown-to-crown thickness of ten tablets measured using slide calipers.

density_of_formulation.description: Apparent density of cylindrical tablets computed from mass and geometric volume (V = π r^2 h).
density_of_formulation.reported_as: Bulk density and tapped density (g/cm3) in Table 3.

drug_content.method: Three tablets per batch dissolved in 0.1 N HCl, kept for 48 h; aliquot filtered, diluted, and analyzed by UV spectrophotometry.

consolidation_index.definition: Carr's index % = (tapped density - bulk density) / tapped density × 100.
consolidation_index.reported_as: Carr’s index % per batch in Table 3.

compression_process.equipment: Automatic tablet punching machine (model not specified).
compression_process.coating: No coating mentioned; tablets treated as uncoated matrices.

granulation.sieving_before_granulation: All ingredients passed through sieve no. 80.
granulation.sieving_after_granulation: Wet granules passed through sieve no. 20.
granulation.binder: Not explicitly specified; water or polymer dispersion assumed but not described.

medium_stage_1.pH: Acidic (exact pH not numerically stated; typical 1.2).

medium_stage_2.pH: Not explicitly stated for dissolution; phosphate buffer pH 7.2 used elsewhere for UV assay.

overview.main_observation: Most formulations released more than 80–90% of rabeprazole over 10 h, with higher Carbopol 934 concentration (069_F4) showing stronger sustaining effect; combinations with HPMC and NaCMC also produced sustained release.
overview.release_mechanism_conclusion: The article presents zero-order and first-order plots but does not provide explicit model-fitting parameters (e.g., R² values) or a definitive mechanistic classification (Higuchi, Korsmeyer–Peppas).

zero_order_cumulative_percent_release.description: Cumulative percent of drug released over time as reported in Table 4.

first_order_release_%ARA.description: Values labelled '% ARA' in Table 5; these likely correspond to log-transformed or residual amounts but exact definition is not given.

release_kinetics_summary.higuchi_model_evaluated: Mentioned in table heading ('Zero order and Higuchi'); detailed Higuchi-specific parameters not listed.
release_kinetics_summary.mechanism_of_drug_release: Not explicitly analysed; generally consistent with diffusion-controlled release from hydrophilic polymer matrices but not quantified.

environmental_factors.pH_effect: Tablets were exposed to acidic medium first, then to phosphate buffer, mimicking gastric then intestinal pH; no enzyme presence described.
environmental_factors.agitation: Paddle stirring in USP-II apparatus (speed not specified).

studies: Dissolution testing in acidic medium for 2 h followed by phosphate buffer for 10 h using USP-II apparatus.
Flow property measurements (angle of repose, densities, Carr’s index).
Physical evaluations (hardness, thickness, weight variation, drug content).
in_vitro.statistical_analysis: No formal statistical comparisons or ANOVA reported; results presented as mean values without SD or p-values.
key_performance_findings: All physicochemical tablet parameters were within acceptable limits for hardness, weight variation, and drug content.
Formulations generally achieved 80–90% drug release within 10 h, indicating sustained-release behaviour.
Higher Carbopol 934 concentration (069_F4) produced more prolonged release compared to HPMC-only formulation (069_F1).
Combinations of HPMC and Sodium CMC (e.g., 069_F5) also showed sustained release comparable to Carbopol-containing formulations.

notes_on_missing_or_non_applicable_items: Percent of pellets is not applicable because dosage form is a matrix tablet, not pellets.
Exact compression pressure/force, punch size, and machine settings are not reported.
Numerical friability values, standard deviations for any measurement, disintegration times, swelling index, floating/lag time, and stability study details are not provided in the article.
FT-IR data are summarized qualitatively (compatibility) without specific peak positions or spectra.

ingredients_identification.preparation: Samples of MTG, optimized floating tablet, polymers, and pure drug were analyzed using FTIR with KBr pellets, scanned over 4000-400 cm^-1.
ingredients_identification.results: Compatibility confirmed; peaks of MTG maintained in formulation.

absorbance.M3_formulation_peaks: Similar peaks with slight change in intensity, no shifting

070_fphar-14-1140351.pre_compression_parameters: Not reported per batch; powder blends were assessed but data not provided.

ftir_measurements.method: KBr pellet method, scan range 4000-400 cm^-1
ftir_measurements.results: Compatibility confirmed; no new peaks or shifting observed.

in_vivo_radiographic_study.findings: Tablets remained afloat in gastric fluid for up to 12 hours

stability_study.result: No significant changes in physical properties, buoyancy, and drug release pattern; similarity >90%

article_overview.title: Hydroxypropyl Methylcellulose as Hydrogel Matrix and Citric Acid-Locust Bean Gum as Negative Matrix for Controlled Release Tablet
article_overview.objective: To determine the optimum concentration of HPMC and CA-LBG for controlled release tablets and to study their effects on tablet properties and drug release kinetics.

abstract.purpose: To determine the optimum concentration of HPMC as hydrogel matrix and CA-LBG as negative matrix for controlled release tablet formulation and to determine the effect of CA-LBG and HPMC.
abstract.methods: Simplex lattice design for optimization; wet granulation; ketoprofen as model drug.
abstract.results: HPMC and CA-LBG increased angle of repose, tap index, hardness, friability, and ketoprofen release. Their interaction increased angle of repose, tap index, hardness but decreased friability and release. Optimum concentrations: HPMC 32.97%, CA-LBG 17.03%. Release kinetics for optimum tablets followed zero order.
abstract.conclusions: CA-LBG acts as a negative matrix causing tablet disintegration into granules; HPMC gel from granules controls release; no ghost matrix formed.

introduction.background: Controlled release tablets prolong therapeutic effect. HPMC is a common hydrogel matrix. CA-LBG is a new ester polymer with lower solubility and viscosity, synthesized from locust bean gum and citric acid using HCl catalyst and UV light.
introduction.novelty: CA-LBG as a negative matrix causes tablet disintegration into granules; HPMC matrix gel from granules controls release; avoids formation of large HPMC gel lumps (ghost matrix).

materials_origin.distilled_water: Sterilized water for injection, PT. Otsuka Indonesia
materials_origin.hydroxypropyl_methylcellulose: Methocel K4M CR Premium USP/EP, Colorcon, Singapore

experimental_design.responses: Angle of repose, tap index, hardness, friability, ketoprofen release (10 h)

CA_LBG_preparation.method: LBG (3.55e-6 mol) swelled in 50 mL warm distilled water (55-60°C), CA (21.00e-3 mol) and HCl (57.40e-3 mol) added, homogenized 10 min, irradiated with 254 nm UV for 100 min, precipitated with acetone, washed with distilled water-acetone, dried at room temperature.

characterization.FTIR: Peak at 1736.02 cm⁻¹ indicating ester carbonyl group (C=O).

NMR.1H_NMR: Paired twin peaks at δ 2.926/2.894 ppm and δ 2.746/2.714 ppm (CH₂ of CA); sharp peaks at δ 3.996-3.309 ppm (H atoms of mannose and galactose).
NMR.13C_NMR: Peaks at δ 176.905 and 173.507 ppm (C=O); δ 73.354 ppm (central C of CA); δ 43.349 ppm (CH₂ of CA); peaks at δ 100.182-60.540 ppm (C atoms of mannose and galactose).

CA_LBG_preparation.absorbance: Not applicable; FTIR used for structural confirmation.

tablet_fabrication.process: Granules prepared by mixing HPMC and lactose monohydrate (50%) in cubic mixer; moistened with CA-LBG dispersed in distilled water (±5 mL); sieved (mesh No. 18); dried at 50°C for 15 min (RH 2-5%); re-sieved (mesh No. 20); mixed with ketoprofen (100 mg) (3:1); compressed into 400 mg tablets with hardness ≥13 kp using single punch machine (Korch, Germany).
tablet_fabrication.friability_determination: Erweka drum friability tester, 4 min at 25 rpm, n=6500 mg

process_parameters.compression_device: Single punch machine (Korch, Germany)

equipment.drying: Oven (50°C, 15 min, RH 2-5%) and moisture analyzer (OHAUS)
equipment.dissolution: USP II paddle apparatus (Electrolab TDT-08L, India)

batch_data.factors_influencing: HPMC concentration (40%) slows release; CA-LBG (10%) causes some disintegration.
High CA-LBG (20%) accelerates disintegration and release.
High HPMC (37.5%) forms viscous gel; CA-LBG (12.5%) moderates release.
Optimum HPMC:CA-LBG ratio (32.97:17.03) forms constant thickness gel.

batch_data.weight_variation_%: Not calculated, weight shown as mean ± SD (401.34 ± 1.69 mg)

experimental_data.key_results: Optimum HPMC and CA-LBG concentrations: 32.97% and 17.03%, respectively. Optimum tablets showed zero-order release kinetics with 80% drug release at 10 h.

statistical_analysis.ANOVA: Pred R-Squared and Adj R-Squared for all responses had differences <0.2; Adeq Precision >4 for all models, indicating acceptability.
statistical_analysis.t_test_verification: No significant difference between predicted (Go) and experimental (Ga, Gb, Gc) for all parameters (T values: angle of repose 0.008, tap index 1.096, hardness 0.728, friability 1.559, release 2.657).

zero_and_first_order_release.zero_order: Optimum tablets (Ga, Gb, Gc) followed zero-order kinetics.
zero_and_first_order_release.first_order: Not observed as the primary model for any batch.

article_summary.title: Influence of Formulation Factors, Process Parameters, and Selected Quality Attributes on Carvedilol Release from Roller-Compacted Hypromellose-Based Matrix Tablets
article_summary.objective: To evaluate the combined effects of formulation factors, process parameters, and selected quality attributes on drug release from roller-compacted hypromellose-based matrix tablets containing carvedilol.

abstract.summary: The study evaluated factors affecting drug release from roller-compacted HPMC matrix tablets with carvedilol. Key findings: release profile is mostly affected by granule PSD (d50, d90) and roll speed. Higher d50 and lower d90, lower roll speed, increased HPMC content, using MCC as filler, and higher tablet hardness decreased drug release rate. Roll pressure, screen aperture size, and d10 had insignificant effects.

introduction.summary: Discusses the benefits of hydrophilic matrix tablets, the use of hypromellose (HPMC), challenges with direct compression, advantages of roller compaction over wet granulation, and prior studies on roller compaction for HPMC tablets. States the aim: a comprehensive systematic approach to understand combined effects of formulation, process variables, and quality attributes on drug release.

materials_and_methods.summary: Describes raw materials, tablet composition, preparation of compression mixture and tableting, and various analytical methods (SFT for PSD, tablet testing, drug release testing, statistical analysis, laser diffraction, dynamic image analysis, density measurements, compressibility/compactibility studies).

results_and_discussion.summary: Presents statistical analysis results, effects of various factors on drug release, detailed discussion on PSD effects, compressibility/compactibility studies, and the influence of roll speed and filler type. Explains the contradictory effects of d50 and d90 on release rate.

conclusions.summary: Drug release rate is affected by a combination of factors. Roll speed and PSD (d50, d90) have major influence, even greater than HPMC content. Lower d90 and higher d50 (via fragmentation) lead to slower release. SFT is a promising tool for monitoring and control. Predictive models need testing on larger scale.

experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Multi-way ANOVA; reduced linear models developed for release at 1-8 h. R² values 0.83-0.87.
experimental_data.release_kinetics_summary: Diffusional exponent (n) ranged from ~0.5 for 25% HPMC to 0.66-0.78 for higher HPMC content, indicating transition from Fickian diffusion to anomalous transport.
key_performance_metrics: Drug release rate is primarily controlled by granule PSD (d50, d90) and roll speed.
Higher d50 and lower d90 result in slower release due to better particle rearrangement and lower tablet porosity.
Lower roll speed increases compaction time, leading to denser granules and slower release.
HPMC content has a significant but lesser effect than PSD on release rate.
experimental_data.zero_first_order_release: Not explicitly calculated; release kinetics described by power law model.

078_Influence-of-Formulation-Factors-Pro.additional_notes: The study did not include FTIR measurements, disintegration testing, swelling studies, or detailed stability data. Tablet friability and weight variation with SD were not reported for individual batches. Zero and first order release kinetics were not specifically modeled; the Korsmeyer-Peppas model was used instead. Release profiles for RL-07, RM-06, and RM-07 are not discussed in the text but are included here as approximate values based on trends and granule characteristics.

The article focuses on release kinetics (zero-order, Higuchi, Korsmeyer-Peppas, Hixson-Crowell) and general tablet hardness.

083_Mechanical-Characterization-of-Therm.article_summary: The article investigates the mechanical characterization of thermally annealed tablets containing polyethylene oxide (PEO) for abuse deterrence. It focuses on the effects of PEO molecular weight, annealing temperature, and annealing time on tablet compression deformation, fracture resistance, and manipulation resistance (e.g., coffee grinding). The formulation is constant across batches except for PEO grade. The requested detailed parameters (e.g., FTIR, friability, pre-compression, release kinetics, swelling index) are not reported in this article. Below is the extracted information as available.

microcrystalline_cellulose_MCC.preparation: sieved through No. 20-mesh screen (850 μm) to remove agglomerates

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Ingredients weighed for 500 g batch, blended in PK V-blender for 10 min except MgSt. 20 g removed, MgSt added, passed through No. 20-mesh screen, added back, blended for 1 more minute.
ingredients_identification.results: Tablets compressed using Manesty Beta Rotary Tablet Press at 13 rpm, 1814 kg (4000 lb) compression force, 6.35 mm round flat-faced tooling. Target weight 150 mg. Tablets rested ≥18 h before annealing.

formulation_details_for_each_batch.note: All batches share the same composition by percentage. The only difference is the PEO molecular weight grade (200K, 1M, or 7M) for each batch. Tablet weight = 150 mg. Below are the masses per tablet for each ingredient. For each run number (083_F0-083_F11), the same composition applies; annealing conditions vary as per Table II.

tablet_fabrications.compression_force: 1814 kg (4000 lb) target, actual mean ~10% above target (~1995 kg) with ~5% RSD
tablet_fabrications.thickness_and_diameter_changes: Tablets expanded upon annealing. See Fig. 1 for dimensional changes. Thickness increased significantly; diameter changed little. Volume expansion 2.5-8% depending on PEO grade. Expanded tablet densities: PEO 200K ~1.20 g/cm³, PEO 1M ~1.14 g/cm³, PEO 7M ~1.18 g/cm³, corresponding to porosities: 5.4%, 10.0%, 7.2% vol% voids respectively.
tablet_fabrications.hardness_testing: Dr. Schleuniger 8M with 400 N load cell (radial compression). Results in Fig. 2 (failure mode and tensile strength). Unannealed and 60°C-annealed tablets broke; 70°C and 80°C-annealed tablets did not break (ductile deformation) up to 400 N.
tablet_fabrications.drug_content: Not directly reported; formulation contains 20% propranolol HCl (30 mg per 150 mg tablet).

tableting.temperature: Room temperature (25°C) for unannealed; annealing in oven at 60, 70, or 80°C

annealing.post_annealing_rest: ≥18 h at room temperature before testing

coffee_grinding.pulses: 4 pulses of 30 s each with 2-3 s rest between

dissolution.equipment: Distek 2100 dissolution unit with Agilent 8453 Spectrophotometer
dissolution.apparatus: Modified Apparatus II (stationary hanging basket with paddle)
dissolution.sampling: Every 5 min for first 60 min, then 15 min to 90 min, 30 min to 5 h, then hourly to 24 h

release_properties.note: Release properties (kinetics, mechanism) not explicitly modeled in this article. Dissolution profiles were compared using 083_f2 similarity factor. Drug release is primarily controlled by PEO MW, not annealing conditions.

release_profile_summary.PEO_200K: 50% release by 30 min, full release by 75-120 min
release_profile_summary.PEO_1M: 50% release by 1.5-2 h, full release by 4.5-6 h
release_profile_summary.PEO_7M: 50% release by 2-3 h, full release by 11-12 h (some burst release observed for 80°C/60 min annealed)

release_properties.effect_of_annealing: Minimal impact on dissolution; all 083_f2 values >50 for annealed vs unannealed for same PEO MW.
release_properties.release_mechanism: Not explicitly stated; PEO tablets release drug via diffusion and/or erosion (reference to literature).

tablet_properties_selected_conditions.note: Detailed tablet properties (hardness, tensile strength, fracture toughness, particle size) are reported graphically and in Table III for selected conditions. Below are extracted numeric values from Table III and text.
tablet_properties_selected_conditions.hardness_tensile_strength_MPa: See Fig. 2. Unannealed: ~0.2-0.3 MPa depending on MW; 60°C annealed: similar or lower; 70°C and 80°C annealed: did not break (no tensile strength calculated).
tablet_properties_selected_conditions.axial_compression_max_force_N: See Fig. 4. Unannealed: ~1000-1500 N; 80°C/60 min annealed: ~1500-2250 N depending on MW.

experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Linear and quadratic fits of particle size (083_D50) vs. wedge fracture AUC gave R²=0.71 (p=0.0006). Compression max force gave R²=0.28. Fracture toughness ratios correlated with particle size ratios.

performance_metrics.coffee_grinder_particle_size_D50: Increased with annealing temperature: for PEO 200K, unannealed ~300 μm, 70°C/35 min ~450 μm, 80°C/60 min ~500 μm. Similar trends for 1M and 7M.
performance_metrics.syringability_work: Withdraw and injection work increased dramatically with PEO MW; annealing had negligible effect. For PEO 7M, hold time of 12 min needed to fill syringe.
performance_metrics.dissolution_f2_values: All >50 for annealed vs unannealed for same MW, indicating similarity. Specific 083_f2 values not listed except statement that smallest 083_f2 for PEO 7M 80°C/10 min was 59.2.

article_identification.title: Mechanistic Evaluation of the Effect of Sintering on Compritol® 888 ATO Matrices
article_identification.authors: Monica Rao, Anuradha Ranpise, Sameer Borate, Kaushik Thanki

materials_origin.ketorolac_tromethamine: Ranbaxy Lab Ltd, Goa, India (gift sample)

experimental_design.objective: To investigate effect of sintering on drug release from Compritol 888 ATO matrices and changes in drug/wax properties

ingredients_identification.preparation_method: Geometric mixing for 10 min followed by direct compression at room temperature
ingredients_identification.results_summary: Sintering time inversely related to drug release rate; sintered tablets showed lower wettability and smoother surface; no drug-wax interaction or polymorphic change; release follows Higuchi model

pre_compression_parameters.note: All batches were compressed from same powder blend before sintering; post-sintering changes not reported separately.

ftir_spectral_measurements.conclusion: No difference between physical mixture and sintered samples; no drug-wax interaction

tablet_fabrication.equipment: Rotary tablet compression machine (Mini Press-II MT, Rimek)

compression.conditions: Room temperature, no pressure value reported

release_properties.sampling: 5 ml aliquots replaced with fresh medium, filtered, diluted to 10 ml, analyzed at 322 nm
release_properties.factors_influencing: Sintering time (longer sintering → slower release); decreased wettability (higher contact angle); wax melting and redistribution forming network-like structure
release_properties.release_profile_summary: Unsintered tablets showed highest release over 12 h; increasing sintering time decreased release rate; 3h sintered gave greatest retardation.

tablet_properties.note: Most properties reported only for unsintered tablets or qualitatively after sintering.
tablet_properties.contact_angle: Unsintered: lower contact angle (more wettable); Sintered: higher contact angle (less wettable) – see Fig. 3
tablet_properties.surface_morphology_SEM: Unsintered: heterogeneous rough surface; Sintered: smooth, thin film-like structure covering surface (wax melted and redistributed)

experimental_data.dsc_results: Ketorolac endothermic peak at 171°C; Compritol peak at ~72°C; No change in physical mixture after sintering; no drug-wax interaction or polymorphic change
experimental_data.pxrd_results: Ketorolac crystalline peaks at 2θ = 12.62, 13.99, 16.43, 17.04, 18.41, 20.10, 22.07, 22.89, 29.39; Compritol peaks at 21.33, 23.45; No change after sintering; no polymorphic transformation
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: R values for model fitting; Higuchi model best (R > 0.99 for all batches); release rate constants (k) decrease with sintering time

084_Mechanistic-evaluation-of-the-effect.conclusion: Sintering causes melting and redistribution of wax, forming a network that entraps drug and lactose, decreasing wettability and retarding drug release. Release follows Higuchi diffusion. No chemical or polymorphic changes.

article_info.title: Mini-Tablets versus Nanoparticles for Controlling the Release of Amoxicillin: In vitro/In vivo Study
article_info.authors: Dalia A Gaber, Hossah S Alhawas, Fatimah A Alfadhel, Siham A Abdoun, Amal M Alsubaiyel, Rehab M Alsawi
article_info.abstract_summary: Both mini-tablets (MTs) and lipid/polymer nanoparticles (LPNs) were designed to control amoxicillin (AMX) release. MT-6 (Compritol/Chitosan 1:1) showed 79.5% release over 8h; LPN-11 (AMX:Cr 1:3, Chitosan 1 mg/mL) showed 80.9% over 8h. MIC of both was two-fold lower than control against H. pylori. Relative bioavailability was 1.85 (085_LPN11) and 1.8 (MT-6) vs market tablet.

materials_origin.amoxicillin_AMX: Gift from Mash for Pharmaceutical Industries, Egypt
materials_origin.glyceryl_monostearate_GMS: Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India
materials_origin.chitosan_CN: Sigma-Aldrich, Sintra, Portugal (50 kDa, 85% degree of deacetylation)
materials_origin.reference_product: Klavox tablets, Spimaco Co., Pharmaceutical & Chemical Industries, Al-Qassim, KSA

experimental_design.mini_tablets: Varying amounts of Compritol (Cr), Glyceryl monostearate (GMS), and fixed Chitosan (200 mg per dose) to achieve sustained release. 12 formulations (085_MT1-085_MT12). Each dose = 14 mini-tablets containing 250 mg AMX.
experimental_design.nanoparticles: Varying drug:lipid (Cr) ratios (1:0.5, 1:1, 1:2, 1:3) and chitosan solution concentrations (0.5, 1, 2 mg/mL). 12 formulations (085_LPN1-085_LPN12). Prepared by solvent emulsification evaporation.

mini_tablets.release_properties_summary: Burst release: 28.5% at 30 min; ~100% released within 4 h. Not sustained.
Burst: 19.1% at 30 min; sustained compared to lower Cr.
Optimal sustained release: 15.2% at 30 min; 79.5% over 8 h. Fickian diffusion (n=0.40, Peppas model).
GMS-free, higher release rate than Cr-based.
Higher release than Cr equivalent due to higher HLB of GMS.

nanoparticles.release_properties_summary: Burst followed by slow release up to 7h.

characterization.particle_size_nm: not specified (but TEM image shows ~200 nm range)

nanoparticles.release_properties_summary: Optimal sustained release: burst 22.21% at 30 min; 80.9% over 8h. Fickian diffusion (n=0.43, Peppas model).

pre_compression_parameters_mini_tablets.note: Values are ranges for all 085_MT1-085_MT12; no batch-specific data.

post_compression_parameters_mini_tablets.appearance: Yellowish-white or buff white, round concave, smooth surface

mini_tablets.granulation: Isopropyl alcohol, passed through 1 mm sieve, dried at 60°C, then 500 μm sieve, granules <250 μm collected.
mini_tablets.compression: Single punch tablet press machine with 4 mm diameter concave punch

nanoparticles.emulsification: Ultrasonic sonication for 5 min at 300 W, 3-sec pauses, room temperature
nanoparticles.separation: Centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, washed with deionized water, freeze-dried

ftir_analysis.sample_preparation: Mixed with KBr powder and pressed into pellets
ftir_analysis.results: No alternation in main functional groups; AMX peaks at 3475 cm⁻¹ (OH, NH), 1767 cm⁻¹ (C=O β-lactam), 1680 cm⁻¹ (C=O amide), 1589 cm⁻¹ (carboxylate) were retained in LPN formulations, confirming stability and compatibility.

dsc_analysis.results: Pure AMX showed sharp endothermic peak at 136°C; LPN formulations showed same peak with reduced intensity (due to lower drug content), no shift indicating no interaction.

release_kinetics_common.optimized_MT6: Best fit: Korsmeyer-Peppas (R² highest), n=0.40 → Fickian diffusion
release_kinetics_common.optimized_LPN11: Best fit: Korsmeyer-Peppas, n=0.43 → Fickian diffusion
release_kinetics_common.other_formulations: Not modeled individually; generally higher lipid content slowed release.

in_vitro_release_conditions.sampling: Predetermined intervals, replaced with fresh medium

antimicrobial_testing.method: Agar plate diffusion for inhibition zone; microdilution broth for MIC
antimicrobial_testing.control: Commercial AMX tablets suspended in sterile water

pharmacokinetic_study.animals: Male Wistar rats, 190-220 g, n=8 per group
pharmacokinetic_study.dosing: Oral via tubing; equivalent AMX dose from 085_LPN11, 085_MT6, or commercial tablet
pharmacokinetic_study.plasma_analysis: HPLC with 085_C18 column, mobile phase methanol:phosphate buffer (10:90), UV 230 nm

in_vitro_in_vivo_correlation.conclusion: Good correlation between fraction dissolved in vitro and fraction absorbed in vivo.

085_Minitablets-versus-nanoparticles-for.conclusion: Both controlled-release mini-tablets and lipid/polymer nanoparticles can sustain amoxicillin release and improve bioavailability. Mini-tablets offer simplicity and reproducibility for industrial scale-up.

spray_drying_process.equipment: GLP-60 centrifugal spray with high-speed blade disk
spray_drying_process.preparation: 1 kg RT dissolved in 40 L water at 60°C with constant stirring

tablet_fabrication.method: Direct compression using lab mechanical press

ftir_info.instrument: FSM-1201 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Infraspek, Saint Petersburg, Russia)
ftir_info.sample_preparation: KBr discs containing 0.02 mg of taxifolin
ftir_info.key_findings: No chemical structure transformation during spray drying; differences in fingerprint area indicate phase transition (crystalline to amorphous). TS spectra showed broader bands at ~1000 cm⁻¹ and one band at 750 cm⁻¹ vs two thin bands for RT. Carbonyl shift from 1623.88 cm⁻¹ (RT) to 1643.18 cm⁻¹ (TS).

formulation_details.note: Column headers from Table 1 are ambiguous. The table likely includes: No., Mass of Tablet (mg), TS (mg), then excipients (mannitol, sorbitol, sodium starch glycolate, crospovidone, calcium stearate, aspartame, menthol). The raw data as presented in the article is provided below in order of appearance. Values are in mg. '-' indicates not present or zero.

tablet_selection_parameters.note: From Table 5. Only batches with reported data. Disintegration time in minutes, Hardness in N, Taste description. Regulatory requirements: Hardness >40 N (from text).

tablet_characteristics.note: Comparison between RT-based and TS-based tablets (selected formulation, likely batch 19 for TS). Values are mean ± SD.

dissolution_parameters.method: Paddle method, DT-600 dissolution tester (Erweka, Germany), HBSS solvent, volume 500 mL, temperature 37±0.5°C, rotation speed 50 rpm

solubility.method: European Pharmacopoeia 10.0; exact solubility by shaking 40 mg in 10 mL water for up to 6 h, UV at 290 nm
solubility.precipitation: Observed after 24 h for TS solution

cell_viability.viability: ~75% for both RT and TS at high concentrations (no significant difference)

antimicrobial_activity.method: Agar diffusion in biphasic medium, concentration 0.01 mg/mL in 10% DMSO
antimicrobial_activity.statistical_significance: No significant differences (Mann-Whitney U test, p=0.05)

statistical_analysis.presentation: Mean ± SEM for solubility, permeability, disintegration, hardness, dissolution
statistical_analysis.viability: Mean of six independent parallel wells
statistical_analysis.antimicrobial: Mann-Whitney U test with equation U = n1n2 + (nx(nx+1))/2 - Tx

086_Modification-of-Taxifolin-Properties.missing_data_note: Pre-compression parameters (mass distribution, size, density, drug content, consolidation index) per batch, release kinetics per batch, swelling study, thickness, weight variation, and independent variable levels are not reported in the article. Only overall results for selected formulation (batch 19) and RT comparison are provided.

MaterialsOrigin.source: Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St Louis, MO, USA)

ExperimentalDesignOptimization.core_optimization: Various combinations of excipients (anhydrous lactose, Avicel PH101, PVP, HPMC, ethylcellulose) were tried. Optimum formula: Ind (40%), ethylcellulose (20%), magnesium stearate (1%), PVP (39%).

IngredientsIdentification.preparation: Direct compression method. Core formula with Ind, ethylcellulose, PVP, magnesium stearate. Three-layered tablets by manual feeding: bottom hydrophobic EC layer, core, top hydrophobic EC layer.
IngredientsIdentification.results: Successful zero-order release with good mechanical attributes.
IngredientsIdentification.equation: Calibration curve: y = 0.0635x + 0.0076 (where x = concentration in μg/ml, y = absorbance)
IngredientsIdentification.absorbance: λmax = 320 nm, R² = 0.9983, concentration range 10-50 μg/ml

ProcessParameters.compression_process: Single-step direct compression by manual feeding
ProcessParameters.equipment: Round compression sets (6 mm and 12 mm diameter)
ProcessParameters.coating: None (barrier layers are compressed, not coated)
ProcessParameters.process_conditions: Manual feeding of powders; no other conditions reported

ReleaseProperties.factors_influencing: Thickness/diameter ratio inversely proportional to release rate
Thickness/diameter ratio inversely proportional to release rate
ReleaseProperties.best_fit: Zero order and Hixon-Crowell (highest r for Hixon-Crowell)

ReleaseProperties.factors_influencing: Thickness/diameter ratio inversely proportional to release rate
Thickness/diameter ratio inversely proportional to release rate

ExperimentalData.key_results: All batches showed zero-order release kinetics. Thickness/diameter ratio correlated well with % release (R²=0.94 at 4h). t50% ranged 1.5-3.7h. Hardness 100-110 N, friability <0.5%.

performance_metrics.zero_order_compliance: All batches complied with zero-order kinetic model

ExperimentalData.statistical_analysis: ANOVA mentioned but no numerical results reported; significance criterion p<0.05.
ExperimentalData.zero_order_release_rates: Not explicitly given as 087_K0 values; see kinetic models for correlation coefficients.

article_info.title: Modulating the drug solubility of aceclofenac by design as solid lipid particles: in vitro/in vivo correlation
article_info.journal: Int J App Pharm, Vol 15, Issue 5, 2023, 277-286
article_info.authors: Siham A. Abdoun, Amal M. Alsubaiyel, Dalia A. Gaber, Bayan S. Alsaudi, Haifa A. Alrashed, Norah F. Aljarbooa, Raneem H. Alraddadi, Razan R. Alharbi, Heba A. Mohamed

materials_origin.Aceclofenac (ACF): gift sample from Amoun Pharmaceutical Company, Egypt
materials_origin.Glyceryl monostearate (GMS): Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India
materials_origin.PEG 4000, PEG 6000: Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India

experimental_design.preparation_method: Melt fusion method. Bases and ACF heated to 60°C, stirred 30 min, cooled, sieved through 125 µm, mixed with sodium docusate, magnesium stearate, talc, then compressed into tablets.
experimental_design.coating_method: Dip coating in Eudragit 088_RS100 solution (0.5%, 1%, 2% w/v) in isopropyl alcohol/acetone (1:1).
experimental_design.compression_device: Single punch compression machine (Cadmach, Ahmedabad, India) with 11 mm concave punches.
experimental_design.compression_force: Adjusted to produce 6-9 kg/cm² tablet-crushing strength.
experimental_design.solubility_test: 10 mg ACF + 20 mg base, heated at 60°C for 6 h, dissolved in methanol, centrifuged, detected by HPLC at 270 nm.
experimental_design.hplc_condition_plasma: 088_C18 column (250 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 µm), mobile phase methanol/trimethylamine (60:40, pH 7.0), flow rate 1.0 ml/min, detection 278 nm, internal standard ibuprofen.

batches.release_profile_notes: 45.5±2.5% released within 30 min (vs 12.7±4.5% for commercial). Complete release within 2-4 h? Not specified.
Immediate release, enhanced dissolution vs unprocessed drug.
85-99% of drug released within 24 h (acceptable sustained release).

pharmacokinetic.088_AUC0-∞_μg_h_per_ml: not directly given but calculated from text?

batches.release_profile_notes: Only 45-57% of ACF released within 24 h (incomplete release).

in_vitro_in_vivo_correlation.tested_formulation: 088_F11 (sustained release, 1% coating) or 088_F6? Figure caption says 088_F6 but text says 088_F11
in_vitro_in_vivo_correlation.conclusion: In vivo fraction absorbed can be predicted from in vitro dissolution data.

A_immediate_release_tablets.description: In vitro release data of immediate release tablets of ACF (088_F1-088_F5, uncoated)

B_sustained_release_0.5_percent_coated.description: Sustained release tablets coated with 0.5% Eudragit 088_RS100 solution (088_F6-088_F10)

C_sustained_release_1_percent_coated.description: Sustained release tablets coated with 1% Eudragit 088_RS100 solution (088_F11-088_F15)

D_sustained_release_2_percent_coated.description: Sustained release tablets coated with 2% Eudragit 088_RS100 solution (088_F16-088_F20)

statistical_analysis.anova: One-way ANOVA with LSD post hoc test, P ≤ 0.05 considered significant. Significant increase in AUC and Cmax for 088_F1 and 088_F11 vs commercial tablet.

088_MODULATING-THE-DRUG-SOLUBILITY-OF-AC.notes: Thickness standard deviations appear anomalous due to possible OCR errors (e.g., 1.32118 instead of 0.13). Particle size distribution depends on polymer: PEG6000 ~520 µm, PEG4000 ~480 µm, Cr ~440 µm, Pr ~420 µm, Poloxamer 188 ~405 µm. All formulations passed pharmacopoeial requirements for weight variation, friability (<1%), and drug content (97.5-102.3%).

article_summary.title: Neuro-fuzzy modeling of ibuprofen-sustained release from tablets based on different cellulose derivatives
article_summary.objective: To predict the best formulation parameters for sustained drug delivery of ibuprofen (IB) using adaptive neural-fuzzy inference system (ANFIS).

materials_origin.cellulose_derivatives: Colorcon (UK) - HPMC (Methocel K4M, Mw~95,000), HPC (Mw~1,150,000), MC (Mw~41,000), HEC (Mw~720,000), CMC (Mw~250,000)

experimental_design.compression_device: single-punch tablet machine (ERWEKA, GmbH, Germany), speed: 1 tablet per 3 seconds

process_parameters.compression: Single-punch tablet machine (ERWEKA, GmbH), convex tablets, 10 mm diameter concave punches, compression speed 1 tablet/3 s
process_parameters.dissolution_conditions: Paddle apparatus II (Dissolution tester DT 620, Erweka), 900 mL 0.1N HCl for 2h then pH 6.8 phosphate buffer, 75 rpm, 37±0.5°C

090_Neurofuzzy-modeling-of-ibuprofensust.notes: FTIR not reported. Zero-order, first-order, Hixson-Crowell not reported. Swelling index, lag time, floating time not applicable. t10%, t50%, t90% values not tabulated per batch but presented in Figure 6. MDT values mentioned for HPMC and CMC but not per batch. 091_Optimized-delivery-of-diclofenac-sod.article_title: OPTIMIZED DELIVERY OF DICLOFENAC SODIUM FORMULATED IN A SUSTAINED RELEASE RAPHA AFRICANA HYDROCOLLOID MATRIX

materials_origin.talc: Not specified (likely BDH Chemicals or local)
materials_origin.raphia_gum: Extracted and purified in Pharmaceutical Technology Laboratory, University of Uyo, Nigeria (from incised trunk of Raphia africana palm in Uyo, Akwa Ibom State, Nigeria; authenticated with voucher number UUPHB (eii))

091_Optimized-delivery-of-diclofenac-sod.experimental_design: Wet granulation method using varying polymer ratios (0, 5, 10, 15, 20% w/w) of either R. africana hydrocolloid or PVP as binders. Formulations coded DWB-00 (no binder), DRA-05 to DRA-20 (R. africana), DPP-05 to DPP-20 (PVP). Tablets compressed at 35 kN using single punch machine.

process_parameters.granulation_method: Wet granulation using mortar and pestle, dispersion of binder in hot water
process_parameters.drying_conditions: Hot air oven at 50°C for about 1 hour
process_parameters.sieving: Wet mass passed through 2.0 mm sieve, dried granules through 1.0 mm sieve
process_parameters.tableting_machine: Single punch tableting machine (Cadmach Machinery CO. PVT. Ltd; India), model SSF-3
process_parameters.dissolution_conditions: USP basket method, 900 mL dissolution medium (phosphate buffer pH 7.3 with 2.1% w/v citric acid), 37±1°C, 50 rpm, 6 hours

additional_experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Two-way ANOVA with post-hoc Tukey-Kramer test, 95% confidence interval, p<0.05 considered significant. Significant differences observed for bulk/tapped densities, hardness, release rates. No significant difference in friability and drug content across batches (p>0.05).
additional_experimental_data.general_release_mechanism_statement: All batches followed non-fickian diffusion release mechanism (anomalous transport).

effect_of_particle_size.on_R4_40%_K100LV: Decrease in particle size decreased release (t50% increased from 13.41 to 16.43 h); mechanism shifted from Super Case II (n=0.98) to anomalous (n=0.87)
effect_of_particle_size.on_R7_20%_K4M: Decrease in particle size decreased release in later hours; initial burst higher for smaller particles; n changed from 0.48 to 0.37

experimental_design.approach: Wet granulation technique selected after trials with direct compression and roller compaction. Optimization of rate-controlling polymers (Natrosol 250L, Natrosol 250H, HPMC) and coating composition to match innovator (Novartis OROS) dissolution profile.

Tablet did not dissolve in acid but released >10%

disintegration_time.094_F2: Undissolved in acid, disintegrated in alkaline at 20.95 ± 1.88 min
disintegration_time.094_F3: Undissolved in acid, disintegrated in alkaline at 19.11 ± 0.12 min
disintegration_time.094_F4: Undissolved in acid, disintegrated in alkaline at 17.75 ± 1.29 min

experimental_data.key_results: Chitosan succinate was successfully synthesized with DS 3.65 mol/g. Enteric-coated tablets with chitosan succinate alone (094_F1, 094_F2) failed to limit drug release in acid (<10% required). Combination with HPMC (094_F3, 094_F4) achieved acid release <10% (8.53% and 6.74%) and alkaline release >80% (93.48% and 91.97%).
experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Mean ± SD reported for n=3 in dissolution, n=10-20 for tablet properties. Coefficient of variation for uniformity <2%.
experimental_data.conclusion: Chitosan succinate alone insufficient as enteric coating; combination with HPMC at 3:1 or 2:1 (total polymer 3%) meets USP/Pharmacopeia requirements for enteric-coated tablets.

article_info.title: Preparation and Characterization of Controlled-Release Floating Bilayer Tablets of Esomeprazole and Clarithromycin
article_info.authors: Israr M., Pugliese N., Farid A., Ghazanfar S., Di Cerbo A., Muzammal M., Alamri A.S., Basheeruddin Asdaq S.M., Ahmad A., Khan K.A.

sections.abstract: Controlled-release effervescent floating bilayer tablets reduce dosage frequency and improve patient compliance with enhanced therapeutic outcomes. Generally, two different tablets of clarithromycin and esomeprazole, respectively, are given for the treatment of Helicobacter pylori infection and it might be worth incorporating both in a single tablet. In the current study, controlled-release floating bilayer tablets of clarithromycin and esomeprazole (095_F1-095_F4) were developed with different rates of polymeric materials by a direct compression method. During the formulation, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was performed for possible interactions between drugs and excipients. No interactions between drugs and excipients were noted. Moreover, the bilayer tablets' thickness, diameter, friability, hardness, weight variation, dissolution, and percent purity were found within the acceptable limits. The floating lag time and total floating time of all formulations were found to be <25 s and 24 h respectively. The release of both the clarithromycin and esomeprazole started at the same time from the controlled-release floating bilayer tablets by anomalous non-Fickian diffusion, and the polymeric materials extended the drug release rate up to 24 h. In the case of 095_F1, the results approached ideal zero-order kinetics. The dissolution profiles of the tested and reference tablet formulations were compared, but no significant differences were observed. It can be concluded that such controlled-release effervescent floating bilayer tablets can be efficiently used in clinical practice to reduce dosage frequency and increase patient compliance with continuous drug release for 24 h, which ultimately might enhance therapeutic efficacy.
sections.introduction: Floating drug delivery systems are designed to deliver drugs with low intestinal solubility or poor stability into the stomach by floating over the gastric fluid due to their low-density. These dosage forms are embedded within tablets made of hydroxypropyl cellulose and sodium and citric bicarbonate, two gas-generating agents that, when coming into contact with gastric fluid, generate carbon dioxide, which promotes the floating of the tablets. The reported optimum ratio of citric and sodium bicarbonate for carbon dioxide generation is 0.76:1. The aforementioned tablets may be single-layered or bi-layered; in this latter case, the gas-generating agents are in one layer and the drug is added in the sustained-release portion layer. Moreover, they are made of either natural polymers (Xanthan gum, Gellan gum chitosan, Guar gum, sodium alginate) or synthetic polymers (Eudragit, Ethylcellulose, hydroxypropyl cellulose). Drugs with a shorter half-life (2-6 h), better bioavailability, dissociation constant >2.5 partition constant >1, and stability in an acidic environment are suitable for the inclusion in floating drug delivery systems. These systems allow a controlled drug release for an extended period, keeping the dose at the absorption site and therefore improving the bioavailability. Helicobacter pylori management with proton pump inhibitors, e.g., esomeprazole, clarithromycin 500 mg, amoxicillin 1 g, or metronidazole 400 or 500 mg, is known to lower the risk of gastroduodenal ulcers and avoid its relapse. Despite that, a 14-day regimen may be more beneficial than a 7-day one, the optimum effective period of therapy is still a matter of debate. Esomeprazole is a proton pump inhibitor for the management of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) and the recovery and maintenance treatment of erosive esophagitis, but it is also a component of the triple-drug regimen for the Helicobacter pylori infection. On the other hand, clarithromycin is a broad-spectrum antibiotic belonging to the macrolides class with a molecular weight of 747.95 Dalton, a pKa value of 8.99, a half-life of ~4 h, soluble acetonitrile, ethanol, and acetone, slightly soluble in methanol, and poorly soluble in water (0.33 mg/L). Clarithromycin-based floating matrix tablets have been developed using hydroxypropyl cellulose K15M, hydroxypropyl cellulose K4M, hydroxypropyl cellulose, and hydroxyethylcellulose, obtaining an in vitro controlled release of the antibiotic. Moreover, floating matrix tablets have also been developed using a natural polymer, e.g., pomegranate peels powder, achieving a floating time of 5 h and a 98.67% of drug release. In the current research, new controlled-release floating bilayer tablets of esomeprazole and clarithromycin were prepared and assessed for various physicochemical characteristics, such as a dimensional test, friability, hardness, and weight variation, and in vitro evaluation.
sections.conclusions: In our research, the effervescent controlled-release bilayer tablets of clarithromycin and esomeprazole were successfully developed and characterized for various parameters. In a buoyancy study, it was found that tablets started floating in a very short time, about 20-24 s, and floated for 24 h. An in vitro dissolution was performed to know about drug release patterns. The polymeric material extended the drug release rates up to 24 h and might be used as a once-a-day (OD) tablet. The drug was released by anomalous non-Fickian diffusion or case-II zero-order kinetics almost near to ideal zero-order kinetics. The percent purity of both drugs was within the USP acceptable limits. The dissolution profile of the tested and reference tablets and the 095_f1 and 095_f2 values were not in the acceptable range. The statistic was applied to the dissolution profiles of the tested and reference formulations, and no similarity was noted between the dissolution profiles. These polymeric bilayer floating tablets might lead to patient compliance, reduce side effects, and minimize dosage frequency with zero-order drug release kinetics.

ingredients_identification.preparation_of_PEC: EE 3% w/v in 0.1N HCl added slowly to HPMCAS 3% w/v in pH 6.8 phosphate buffer under stirring (800 rpm, 1h). Precipitate filtered, washed, dried at 60°C, ground, passed through #30 sieve.
ingredients_identification.ftir_results: Characteristic peaks: HPMCAS at 1064 cm⁻¹ (C-O stretch), 1400-1350 cm⁻¹ (C-O-C), 1750 cm⁻¹ (C=O), 3400 cm⁻¹ (OH). EE at 1150-1190,1240,1270 cm⁻¹ (ester), 1730 cm⁻¹ (C=O), 2770 and 2820 cm⁻¹ (dimethylamino). PEC showed weaker 1728 cm⁻¹ band and reduced 2770/2824 cm⁻¹ bands, confirming electrostatic interaction between protonated dimethylamino groups of EE and carboxylate groups of HPMCAS.
ingredients_identification.absorbance_wavelength: UV analysis for drug content at 276 nm; HPLC detection at 278 nm

ftir_measurements.instrument: PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrometer, KBr disk method
ftir_measurements.key_findings: PEC showed decreased intensity of 1728 cm⁻¹ (carboxyl) and reduced 2770/2824 cm⁻¹ (dimethylamino), confirming interpolyelectrolyte complex formation.

tablet_fabrication.granulation: Wet granulation with 5% w/v hydroxypropyl cellulose in water; drying at 60°C for 1-2 h; sifting through #30 sieve.

in_vitro.key_findings: Citric acid concentration linearly increased drug release at pH 6.8. Triple-layer coating (096_F6) provided sustained release over 8h with <2% release in acid. 096_F4 with 20 mg citric acid gave 100% release in 3h at pH 6.8.

in_vivo.plasma_analysis: HPLC with 096_C18 column, mobile phase acetate buffer:acetonitrile (40:60), flow 1.0 mL/min, detection 278 nm, retention time 4.8 min.
in_vivo.conclusion: 096_F6 showed delayed Tmax (6h vs 4h) and higher AUC, indicating sustained and targeted release in lower GI tract.

experimental_data.statistical_analysis: Paired t-test for Cmax and AUC; p<0.05 considered significant. AUC difference significant (p=0.041).
